REGISTER: fcsr
NUMBER: 0x003
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: 32
FIELDS: 7..5=FRM, 4..4=NV, 3..3=DZ, 2..2=OF, 1..1=UF, 0..0=NX
EXTENSIONS: F, Zdinx, Zfinx
RULE 1: The fcsr CSR is private to a hart and is not visible to other harts.
RULE 2: Accesses to the fcsr may be freely reordered in the global memory order with respect to FENCE instructions without violating this specification.
RULE 3: The fcsr CSR is defined with bit‑field granularity comprising bits 7‑5, bit 4, bit 3, bit 2, bit 1, and bit 0.
RULE 4: The fflags CSR is an alias of bits 4‑0 of fcsr, so any access to fflags reads or writes those bits in fcsr.
RULE 5: The frm CSR is an alias of bits 7‑5 of fcsr, so any access to frm reads or writes those bits in fcsr.
RULE 6: Any write to fcsr updates both the fflags sub‑field (bits 4‑0) and the frm sub‑field (bits 7‑5) within the same register.
RULE 7: For syntactic dependency tracking, each individual bit of fcsr (bits 7‑5, 4, 3, 2, 1, and 0) is treated as an independent unit, and a read or write to any one of those bits, whether via fcsr directly or through its fflags or frm aliases, creates a distinct dependency.
RULE 8: The floating‑point control and status register fcsr is defined as part of the RISC‑V "F" extension.
RULE 9: The fcsr register contains the operating mode and the exception status of the floating‑point unit.
RULE 10: The RISC‑V "F" extension adds thirty‑two floating‑point registers (f0‑f31) and also adds the floating‑point control and status register fcsr.
RULE 11: In the architectural state diagram the fcsr register appears as the final element of the floating‑point state block, positioned after register f31.
RULE 12: The fcsr register occupies bits 31 down to 0 of the floating‑point state block.
RULE 13: The fcsr is a 32‑bit read/write control‑status register.
RULE 14: Bits 7‑5 of the fcsr constitute the rounding‑mode field (frm).
RULE 15: Bits 4‑0 of the fcsr constitute the accrued‑exception flags field (fflags).
RULE 16: Bits 31‑8 of the fcsr are reserved for optional standard extensions.
RULE 17: The FRCSR instruction reads the entire fcsr and writes its value to the integer register specified as rd.
RULE 18: The FSCSR instruction atomically swaps the whole fcsr with the integer register rs1, returning the previous fcsr value in rd.
RULE 19: The FRRM instruction reads the frm field of the fcsr, places its three bits into the least‑significant bits of rd, and zero‑extends the remaining bits of rd.
RULE 20: The FSRM instruction swaps the frm field of the fcsr with the three least‑significant bits of rs1, returning the previous frm value in rd.
RULE 21: The FRFLAGS instruction reads the fflags field of the fcsr, places its five bits into the least‑significant bits of rd, and zero‑extends the remaining bits of rd.
RULE 22: The FSFLAGS instruction swaps the fflags field of the fcsr with the five least‑significant bits of rs1, returning the previous fflags value in rd.
RULE 23: An implementation that does not provide the extensions associated with bits 31‑8 shall ignore any writes to those bits of the fcsr.
RULE 24: When bits 31‑8 are not implemented, reads of the fcsr shall return zero for those bits.
RULE 25: Software that modifies the fcsr shall preserve the contents of bits 31‑8.
RULE 26: The dynamic rounding mode used by floating‑point arithmetic instructions is taken from the frm field of the fcsr.
RULE 27: The frm field of the fcsr is encoded as follows: 000 = round‑to‑nearest‑ties‑to‑even, 001 = round‑toward‑zero, 010 = round‑down, 011 = round‑up, 100 = round‑to‑nearest‑ties‑to‑max‑magnitude, 101 and 110 are reserved, and 111 is reserved as a value of the frm field.
RULE 28: The value 111 in an instruction’s rm field selects the dynamic rounding mode held in the frm field of the fcsr.
RULE 29: The fflags field of the fcsr records, in bits 4‑0, the exception conditions that have occurred on any floating‑point arithmetic instruction since the field was last cleared by software.
RULE 30: The base RISC‑V ISA does not generate a trap when a bit in the fflags field of the fcsr is set; software must explicitly test the fflags.
RULE 31: Writing to the fflags field of the fcsr replaces only its five least‑significant bits; higher bits of the written value are ignored.
RULE 32: Writing to the frm field of the fcsr replaces only its three bits (7‑5); other bits of the written value are ignored.
RULE 33: In a system that implements the Zfinx extension, the mstatus.FS field being hard‑wired to 0 does not affect the trapping behavior of any fcsr register access.
RULE 34: The floating‑point control and status register fcsr is included in the architectural floating‑point state whose status is tracked by the FS field in mstatus.
RULE 35: The FS field in mstatus encodes the status of the floating‑point unit, and this encoding includes the state of the fcsr.
RULE 36: If an instruction explicitly or implicitly writes the fcsr without changing its value while the FS field is Initial or Clean, it is implementation‑defined whether the FS field transitions to Dirty.
RULE 37: The FCSR bit in each state‑enable CSR (mstateen0, hstateen0, sstateen0) controls access to the fcsr register when floating‑point instructions are defined to operate on integer registers by the Zfinx or Zdinx extensions.
RULE 38: When the F bit in the misa register is set (misa.F = 1), the FCSR bit of mstateen0 is permanently read‑only zero.
RULE 39: When the F bit in the misa register is set (misa.F = 1), the FCSR bits of hstateen0 and sstateen0 are also permanently read‑only zero.
RULE 40: If misa.F = 0 and the state‑enable CSRs are implemented, and the FCSR bit of the controlling stateen0 CSR for the current privilege level is zero, every floating‑point instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception (or a virtual‑instruction exception where applicable).
RULE 41: When the condition in RULE 4 occurs, the exception is generated as if the executed instruction accessed the fcsr register, even when the instruction does not actually reference fcsr.
RULE 42: In M-mode, accessing fcsr register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x003; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 43: In S-mode, accessing fcsr register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x003; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 44: In U-mode, accessing fcsr register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x003; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 45: In M-mode, setting fcsr.FRM (bit 5) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x20) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x20; csrs 0x003, a0; csrr a1, 0x003; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x20].
RULE 46: In M-mode, setting fcsr.NV (bit 4) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10; csrs 0x003, a0; csrr a1, 0x003; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10].
RULE 47: In M-mode, setting fcsr.DZ (bit 3) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8; csrs 0x003, a0; csrr a1, 0x003; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8].
RULE 48: In M-mode, setting fcsr.OF (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x003, a0; csrr a1, 0x003; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 49: In M-mode, setting fcsr.UF (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x003, a0; csrr a1, 0x003; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 50: In M-mode, setting fcsr.NX (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x003, a0; csrr a1, 0x003; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 51: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the fcsr register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x003, a0; csrr a1, 0x003; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 52: In M-mode, accessing fcsr register while F extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: F; csrr a0, 0x003; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 53: In M-mode, accessing fcsr register while Zdinx extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zdinx; csrr a0, 0x003; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 54: In M-mode, accessing fcsr register while Zfinx extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zfinx; csrr a0, 0x003; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: fflags
NUMBER: 0x001
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: 32
FIELDS: 4..4=NV, 3..3=DZ, 2..2=OF, 1..1=UF, 0..0=NX
EXTENSIONS: F, V
RULE 1: The bits 4, 3, 2, 1, and 0 of the fflags register are tracked as independent units for syntactic dependencies.
RULE 2: The fflags register is an alias of the fcsr register, so any read or write to fflags accesses the same architectural state as fcsr.
RULE 3: The fflags register is not read‑only, allowing software to write values to it.
RULE 4: The fflags register is a control‑and‑status register accessible via CSR instructions.
RULE 5: The fflags register occupies bits 4‑0 of the floating‑point control and status register (fcsr).
RULE 6: The FRFLAGS instruction reads the fflags field and copies its value into the least‑significant five bits of integer register rd, setting all other bits of rd to zero.
RULE 7: The FSFLAGS instruction swaps the fflags field by first copying its current value into integer register rd and then writing the five least‑significant bits of integer register rs1 into the fflags field.
RULE 8: The fflags bits indicate the exception conditions that have arisen on any floating‑point arithmetic instruction since the field was last reset by software.
RULE 9: Bit 0 of fflags (NV) is set when an invalid operation exception occurs.
RULE 10: Bit 1 of fflags (DZ) is set when a divide‑by‑zero exception occurs.
RULE 11: Bit 2 of fflags (OF) is set when an overflow exception occurs.
RULE 12: Bit 3 of fflags (UF) is set when an underflow exception occurs.
RULE 13: Bit 4 of fflags (NX) is set when an inexact result exception occurs.
RULE 14: The base RISC‑V ISA does not automatically generate a trap when any fflags bit is set; software must explicitly test the fflags field to detect floating‑point exceptions.
RULE 15: Writing a zero value to the fflags field clears all accrued exception flags.
RULE 16: A vector floating‑point exception that occurs in any active floating‑point element causes the corresponding standard floating‑point exception flags to be set in the fflags register.
RULE 17: A vector floating‑point exception that occurs in an inactive floating‑point element does not cause any floating‑point exception flags to be set in the fflags register.
RULE 18: The fflags register is a control‑status register that belongs to the floating‑point unit state.
RULE 19: The FS field of the mstatus register encodes the status of the floating‑point unit, and that status includes the fflags register.
RULE 20: In M-mode, accessing fflags register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x001; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 21: In S-mode, accessing fflags register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x001; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 22: In U-mode, accessing fflags register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x001; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 23: In M-mode, setting fflags.NV (bit 4) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x10) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x10; csrs 0x001, a0; csrr a1, 0x001; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x10].
RULE 24: In M-mode, setting fflags.DZ (bit 3) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8; csrs 0x001, a0; csrr a1, 0x001; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8].
RULE 25: In M-mode, setting fflags.OF (bit 2) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x4) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x4; csrs 0x001, a0; csrr a1, 0x001; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x4].
RULE 26: In M-mode, setting fflags.UF (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x001, a0; csrr a1, 0x001; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 27: In M-mode, setting fflags.NX (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x001, a0; csrr a1, 0x001; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 28: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the fflags register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x001, a0; csrr a1, 0x001; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 29: In M-mode, accessing fflags register while F extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: F; csrr a0, 0x001; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 30: In M-mode, accessing fflags register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0x001; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: frm
NUMBER: 0x002
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: 32
FIELDS: 2..0=ROUNDINGMODE
EXTENSIONS: D, F, Q, V
RULE 1: For syntactic dependency analysis, the frm CSR is treated as a whole‑register unit rather than as separate bit fields.
RULE 2: Any instruction that reads or writes any part of frm is considered to read or write the entire frm register.
RULE 3: The frm CSR is an alias of the fcsr CSR, and reads of frm return the same value as reads of fcsr.
RULE 4: The frm CSR is an alias of the fcsr CSR, and writes to frm modify the same underlying hardware state as writes to fcsr.
RULE 5: Because read‑only CSRs are omitted from the dependency table, frm is not classified as a read‑only CSR.
RULE 6: The frm field occupies bits 7‑5 of the floating‑point control and status register fcsr.
RULE 7: The FRRM instruction reads the frm field and copies its three least‑significant bits into the destination integer register rd while writing zeros to all other bits of rd.
RULE 8: The FSRM instruction first copies the current frm value to the destination integer register rd and then overwrites frm with the three least‑significant bits of the source integer register rs1.
RULE 9: Floating‑point instructions whose rm field is set to 111 select the dynamic rounding mode held in frm.
RULE 10: The rounding behavior of a floating‑point instruction that uses the dynamic rounding mode is determined by the value currently stored in frm.
RULE 11: When frm contains the binary value 000 it specifies the rounding mode RNE (round to nearest, ties to even).
RULE 12: When frm contains the binary value 001 it specifies the rounding mode RTZ (round towards zero).
RULE 13: When frm contains the binary value 010 it specifies the rounding mode RDN (round down toward –∞).
RULE 14: When frm contains the binary value 011 it specifies the rounding mode RUP (round up toward +∞).
RULE 15: When frm contains the binary value 100 it specifies the rounding mode RMM (round to nearest, ties to max magnitude).
RULE 16: The binary values 101 and 110 in frm are reserved for future use and must be treated as reserved.
RULE 17: The binary value 111 in frm is reserved in the rounding‑mode register and must be treated as reserved.
RULE 18: Executing a floating‑point instruction while frm holds any reserved encoding (101, 110, or 111) results in reserved behavior.
RULE 19: The current specification does not require an illegal‑instruction exception for a reserved dynamic rounding mode in frm, though implementations may still raise such an exception.
RULE 20: Writes to frm may serialize the pipeline in typical implementations.
RULE 21: Software must preserve the contents of frm when saving and restoring fcsr if the rounding mode is required to remain unchanged.
RULE 22: The frm CSR stores a rounding‑mode encoding that can be supplied to floating‑point operations.
RULE 23: When a scalar floating‑point instruction’s rm field is set to the DYN encoding (binary 111), the instruction uses the rounding mode currently stored in the frm CSR.
RULE 24: The rounding mode applied by each floating‑point instruction is fetched from the frm register.
RULE 25: The frm register holds the current floating‑point rounding‑mode that controls rounding of floating‑point operations.
RULE 26: The sum produced by the vfwmaccbf16 instruction is rounded using the rounding mode specified in the frm register.
RULE 27: All vector floating‑point instructions obtain their dynamic rounding mode from the frm register.
RULE 28: The frm register supplies the rounding mode that controls every vector floating‑point arithmetic operation.
RULE 29: If the frm register contains an invalid rounding‑mode encoding, the effect of any vector floating‑point instruction is reserved, even when the vector length is zero or when vstart ≥ vl.
RULE 30: Vector floating‑point software must assume that the frm register holds a valid rounding‑mode value.
RULE 31: An implementation may raise an exception on any vector floating‑point instruction when the frm register encodes an invalid rounding mode.
RULE 32: For any floating‑point conversion instruction that does not specify round‑towards‑zero, the rounding mode applied is the current dynamic rounding mode stored in the frm register.
RULE 33: For any floating‑point conversion instruction that specifies round‑towards‑zero, the instruction ignores the frm register and always rounds toward zero.
RULE 34: The frm register is a floating‑point control and status register that belongs to the floating‑point unit state together with the fcsr and fflags registers.
RULE 35: The FS[1:0] field of the mstatus register encodes the status of the entire floating‑point unit state, which includes the frm register.
RULE 36: Any modification of the frm register causes the FS field in mstatus to be updated to reflect the new floating‑point unit state.
RULE 37: Privileged code must examine the FS field to determine whether the frm register, along with the rest of the floating‑point state, must be saved or restored during a context switch.
RULE 38: The floating‑point unit state, including the frm register, is always initialized, saved, and restored using the standard floating‑point instructions (F, D, and/or Q).
RULE 39: When accessing the frm register, privileged software must respect the current FS status and act only in accordance with that status.
RULE 40: In M-mode, accessing frm register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x002; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 41: In S-mode, accessing frm register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x002; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 42: In U-mode, accessing frm register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x002; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 43: In M-mode, setting frm.ROUNDINGMODE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x002, a0; csrr a1, 0x002; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 44: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the frm register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x002, a0; csrr a1, 0x002; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 45: In M-mode, accessing frm register while D extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: D; csrr a0, 0x002; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 46: In M-mode, accessing frm register while F extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: F; csrr a0, 0x002; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 47: In M-mode, accessing frm register while Q extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Q; csrr a0, 0x002; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 48: In M-mode, accessing frm register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0x002; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: vcsr
NUMBER: 0x00f
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: MXLEN
FIELDS: 2..1=VXRM, 0..0=VXSAT
EXTENSIONS: V
RULE 1: The vcsr register is located at CSR address 0x00F.
RULE 2: The vcsr register is accessible with unprivileged read‑write privilege (URW).
RULE 3: The vcsr register provides the vector control and status functionality defined by the RISC‑V architecture.
RULE 4: CSR numbers 0x00B‑0x00E are tentatively reserved for future vector CSRs and may be mirrored into the vcsr register.
RULE 5: Reads or writes to any future vector CSR whose number falls in the range 0x00B‑0x00E may affect the contents of the vcsr register.
RULE 6: The vcsr register contains a rounding‑mode field that reflects the current value of the vector fixed‑point rounding‑mode register vxrm.
RULE 7: The rounding‑mode field in vcsr must hold the identical two‑bit value found in vxrm[1:0].
RULE 8: The vcsr register contains a mirrored copy of the vxsat single‑bit saturation flag, so its corresponding bit always has the same value as vxsat[0].
RULE 9: The vcsr register is an XLEN‑bit‑wide vector control and status CSR.
RULE 10: Bits XLEN‑1 down to 3 of the vcsr register are reserved and have no defined function.
RULE 11: Bits 2 and 1 of the vcsr register form the vxrm field that specifies the fixed‑point rounding mode.
RULE 12: Bit 0 of the vcsr register is the vxsat field that indicates the fixed‑point accrued saturation flag.
RULE 13: The vector rounding mode and accrued saturation flag can be read from or written to the vcsr register via the vxrm and vxsat fields.
RULE 14: vcsr is a control‑status register defined by the RISC‑V V extension.
RULE 15: vcsr is one of the CSRs whose state is considered part of the vector extension state, alongside vxrm, vxsat, vstart, vl, vtype, and vlenb.
RULE 16: The VS[1:0] field in the mstatus register encodes the status of the entire vector extension state, including the vcsr register.
RULE 17: The VS field uses the four‑value encoding Off = 0, Initial = 1, Clean = 2, Dirty = 3 to indicate whether the vector registers and vector CSRs such as vcsr are off, in the initial state, clean, or dirty.
RULE 18: Changing the VS field never modifies the value stored in vcsr.
RULE 19: In M-mode, accessing vcsr register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x00f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 20: In S-mode, accessing vcsr register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x00f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 21: In U-mode, accessing vcsr register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x00f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 22: In M-mode, setting vcsr.VXRM (bit 1) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x2) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x2; csrs 0x00f, a0; csrr a1, 0x00f; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x2].
RULE 23: In M-mode, setting vcsr.VXSAT (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x00f, a0; csrr a1, 0x00f; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 24: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the vcsr register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x00f, a0; csrr a1, 0x00f; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 25: In M-mode, accessing vcsr register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0x00f; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: vl
NUMBER: 0xc20
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: MXLEN
FIELDS: 63..0=VALUE
EXTENSIONS: V, Zvkt
RULE 1: The vl register is a vector‑extension CSR located at address 0xC20.
RULE 2: The vl register is accessible at the unprivileged read‑only privilege level (URO).
RULE 3: The vl register holds the current vector length used by vector instructions.
RULE 4: The vl register can be read but cannot be written by any privilege level.
RULE 5: The vl register is a read‑only CSR whose physical width is XLEN bits.
RULE 6: Software cannot write vl directly; vl is updated only by the vset{i}vl{i} instructions and by the fault‑only‑first vector load instruction variants.
RULE 7: The value stored in vl is an unsigned integer that specifies the number of vector elements to be updated by a vector instruction.
RULE 8: The implemented width of vl (the number of bits that actually hold the value) is determined by the implementation’s maximum vector length for the smallest supported element type.
RULE 9: In an implementation where VLEN = 32 and the smallest supported SEW is 8, vl must have at least six implemented bits so that it can represent the range 0 – 32.
RULE 10: After reset, the vector extension must present a consistent architectural state that includes a vl register whose contents are readable.
RULE 11: The value held in vl at reset must be restorable to the same value with a single vsetvl instruction.
RULE 12: The recommended reset value for vl is zero.
RULE 13: No constraints other than readability and single‑instruction restorability are imposed on vl at reset.
RULE 14: An element x is classified as a body element if and only if its index satisfies vstart ≤ x < vl.
RULE 15: An element x is classified as a tail element if and only if its index satisfies vl ≤ x < max(VLMAX, VLEN/SEW).
RULE 16: If vstart is greater than or equal to vl, then no body elements exist for the instruction.
RULE 17: If vstart is greater than or equal to vl, then no destination vector register group is updated.
RULE 18: If vstart is greater than or equal to vl, then tail elements are not written with agnostic values, suppressing the agnostic‑write behavior.
RULE 19: If vl equals zero, then no elements, including any agnostic elements, are updated in any destination vector register group regardless of the value of vstart.
RULE 20: If vl equals zero, then the no‑update rule applies equally to both body‑type processing and tail‑agnostic writes.
RULE 21: In the element‑classification pseudocode, the predicate body(x) is defined as (vstart ≤ x < vl), directly using the current vl value.
RULE 22: In the element‑classification pseudocode, the predicate tail(x) is defined as (vl ≤ x < max(VLMAX, VLEN/SEW)), directly using the current vl value.
RULE 23: An element is considered active if it satisfies both body(x) (which requires vstart ≤ x < vl) and the mask condition.
RULE 24: The vector length register vl is a CSR that holds the actual number of vector elements the hardware will process in each vector‑length iteration.
RULE 25: The application supplies a candidate vector length (AVL) via a general‑purpose register rs1 or an unsigned immediate uimm, which the hardware may use to determine vl.
RULE 26: After executing a configuration‑setting instruction, the hardware may set vl to a value less than or equal to the supplied AVL based on implementation limits and the current vtype setting.
RULE 27: The vsetvli instruction sets vl (and vtype) according to the AVL provided in rs1 and the vtypei operand, and writes the resulting vl value into the destination register rd.
RULE 28: The vsetivli instruction sets vl (and vtype) according to the unsigned immediate uimm (AVL) and the vtypei operand, and writes the resulting vl value into the destination register rd.
RULE 29: The vsetvl instruction sets vl (and vtype) according to the AVL provided in rs1 and the new vtype value supplied in rs2, and writes the resulting vl value into the destination register rd.
RULE 30: The newly computed vl value is returned in the architectural register rd, making the exact vector length visible to software.
RULE 31: In strip‑mining loops, the application tracks remaining elements with AVL while the hardware‑chosen vl determines how many elements are processed in each iteration.
RULE 32: If the vtype value is not supported by the implementation, the processor sets the vl register to zero.
RULE 33: When the vill bit in vtype is set, the vl register must be zero.
RULE 34: The vector‑length register vl holds the current vector length and its value can be read from the vl CSR.
RULE 35: When a vsetvli or vsetvl instruction executes with rs1 ≠ x0, the unsigned integer contained in the register specified by rs1 is taken as the AVL and the computed new vector length is written to vl.
RULE 36: When a vsetvli or vsetvl instruction executes with rs1 = x0 and rd ≠ x0, the AVL is defined as the maximum unsigned integer value and the resulting VLMAX for the current vtype is written to vl.
RULE 37: When a vsetvli or vsetvl instruction executes with rs1 = x0 and rd = x0, the current value of vl is treated as the AVL and the resulting vector length is written to vl only, provided that the new SEW/LMUL configuration does not change VLMAX; otherwise the operation is reserved, and if vill was 1 before the instruction the implementation may set vill again.
RULE 38: If a change in SEW/LMUL would cause VLMAX (and therefore vl) to shrink, the effect on vl is reserved and implementations may assume vl remains unchanged for micro‑architectural optimization.
RULE 39: The vsetivli instruction encodes the AVL as a zero‑extended 5‑bit immediate, and the computed vector length is written to vl following the same rules that apply to vsetvli and vsetvl.
RULE 40: If the active vector length (AVL) supplied to a vsetvl instruction is less than or equal to the computed VLMAX, then vl is set equal to AVL.
RULE 41: If AVL is less than two times VLMAX, then vl is set to a value that satisfies ceil(AVL/2) ≤ vl ≤ VLMAX.
RULE 42: If AVL is greater than or equal to two times VLMAX, then vl is set equal to VLMAX.
RULE 43: For any given implementation, the value written to vl by a vsetvl instruction is deterministic for the same pair of input values AVL and VLMAX.
RULE 44: vl is zero when AVL is zero.
RULE 45: vl is greater than zero when AVL is greater than zero.
RULE 46: vl never exceeds VLMAX.
RULE 47: vl never exceeds AVL.
RULE 48: If the current vl value is read and then used as the AVL argument to a subsequent vsetvl instruction, and the VLMAX computed for that new instruction equals the VLMAX that existed when the original vl was read, then the new vl will be identical to the value that was read.
RULE 49: The vsetvli instruction writes the current vector length, denoted as vl, into the specified destination integer register.
RULE 50: The value of vl produced by vsetvli equals the number of vector elements that will be processed in that iteration.
RULE 51: After vsetvli execution, the scalar register holding vl may be read by subsequent scalar instructions.
RULE 52: A scalar instruction that subtracts the value in a register containing vl reduces the remaining element count by exactly vl.
RULE 53: Architecturally, vl represents the current vector length for the active iteration of a vector operation.
RULE 54: For the additional unit‑stride mask load and store instructions, the effective vector length evl is defined as the ceiling of vl divided by eight (evl = ceil(vl/8)).
RULE 55: A mask load instruction (vlm.v) transfers a byte vector whose length is the ceiling of vl divided by eight.
RULE 56: A mask store instruction (vsm.v) transfers a byte vector whose length is the ceiling of vl divided by eight.
RULE 57: For vlm.v and vsm.v, the vstart register is interpreted in units of bytes, using the current value of vl.
RULE 58: The mask load and store instructions honor the vill field in vtype indirectly by applying the constraints that vill imposes on vl.
RULE 59: When vill is set, the execution of vlm.v and vsm.v follows the illegal‑value semantics associated with an illegal vtype, based on the resulting vl.
RULE 60: If a unit‑stride fault‑only‑first load raises a synchronous exception on element 0, the vl register is not modified and a trap is taken.
RULE 61: If a synchronous exception occurs on any element index greater than 0 of a fault‑only‑first load, vl is reduced to that element’s index.
RULE 62: The vl CSR can be read with a CSRR instruction, and the value returned is the current vector length, i.e., the number of elements that will be processed (or have been processed) by the most recent vector instruction.
RULE 63: Implementations may process fewer than the current vl elements and may consequently reduce vl, provided that when vstart = 0 and vl > 0 at least one element is processed.
RULE 64: When a fault‑only‑first instruction traps because of an interrupt, vl must not be reduced; instead a vstart value is set to indicate the resume point.
RULE 65: When a fault‑only‑first instruction would cause a debug data‑watchpoint trap on an element after the first, vl must not be reduced and the debug trap must be taken so the event is not lost.
RULE 66: Fault‑only‑first load instructions may update active destination elements beyond the element that caused vl trimming, but they must never write past the original vl value before trimming.
RULE 67: The vl register defines the number of segments that a vector load‑store segment instruction will move.
RULE 68: For segment instructions, the value held in vl equals the number of elements transferred to each vector‑register group.
RULE 69: Masking for segment load and store instructions is applied at the granularity of whole segments, and the vl register determines how many segments are processed.
RULE 70: The vl register determines the scope of masking for segment load and store instructions.
RULE 71: For fault‑only‑first unit‑stride segment load instructions, if an exception is detected partway through accessing a segment whose index is greater than zero, the architectural vector‑length register vl is set to the index of that segment.
RULE 72: If an exception is detected partway through accessing the zeroth segment of a fault‑only‑first unit‑stride segment load, a trap is taken and the architectural vector‑length register vl may be reduced.
RULE 73: The amount by which vl may be reduced when an exception occurs in the zeroth segment is implementation‑defined.
RULE 74: When an exception occurs in either the zeroth or a later segment, it is implementation‑defined whether only a subset of the segment is loaded, and the instruction may write destination vector‑register‑group elements beyond the point at which vl is trimmed or the trap is reported.
RULE 75: vl is a control‑status register that holds the current vector length.
RULE 76: The current vector length held in vl can be read with a CSR‑read instruction such as `csrr rd, vl`.
RULE 77: The value stored in vl is used by the vector unit to define the active element range for ordinary vector instructions.
RULE 78: Whole‑register load and store instructions compute an effective vector length (evl) as NFIELDS × VLEN / EEW and ignore the current settings in vl and vtype.
RULE 79: For whole‑register loads and stores the rule "no elements are written if vstart ≥ vl" does not apply.
RULE 80: For whole‑register loads and stores no elements are written when vstart ≥ evl, independent of vl.
RULE 81: Software may preserve the architectural state of vl across code that changes vector configuration by reading vl with a CSR‑read instruction and later restoring it with vsetvli.
RULE 82: Restoring a previously saved vl value is performed by executing `vsetvli x0, <saved value>, <new type>`.
RULE 83: Any vector instruction that is affected by the vector‑length register vl must have a control dependency on vl instead of a data dependency.
RULE 84: The execution of an instruction affected by the vector‑length register vl must rely on the current value of vl for control‑flow decisions and must not treat vl as a source operand supplying data.
RULE 85: If vl equals zero, any vector‑floating‑point instruction that accesses the frm field executes in a reserved manner.
RULE 86: When vl is zero, vector‑floating‑point instructions that do not depend on the rounding mode are still subject to the reservation on using the frm field.
RULE 87: The reservation on using the frm field also applies whenever vstart is greater than or equal to vl.
RULE 88: The current vector length is stored in the vl register.
RULE 89: For vector‑integer merge instructions, the operation is performed on the body elements defined by the current vector length held in vl.
RULE 90: The body elements for vector‑integer merge instructions are the element indices i that satisfy vstart ≤ i < vl.
RULE 91: For the vector‑scalar floating‑point merge instruction, processing includes every element whose index i satisfies i ≥ vstart and i < vl.
RULE 92: The vector‑scalar floating‑point merge instruction disregards the mask value and determines the elements to operate on solely from the current vector length held in vl.
RULE 93: When vl = 0, a vector reduction operation performs no operation and does not update the destination register.
RULE 94: The no‑operation behavior for vl = 0 is defined to simplify implementation and to be consistent with other operations on vector register state.
RULE 95: If the source and destination scalar operands are located in different vector registers, the instruction does not copy the source scalar into the destination when vl = 0.
RULE 96: Software that requires the source scalar to be copied to the destination when vl may be zero must test that vl ≠ 0 before executing the reduction.
RULE 97: Software may achieve the copy semantics for a zero‑vl case by explicitly setting vl = 1 and performing a register‑register copy.
RULE 98: The vector‑length register vl specifies that only elements with indices from 0 up to vl‑1 are active in a vector operation, while elements with index ≥ vl are inactive.
RULE 99: In an ordered sum reduction, the hardware accesses the source vector vs2 exclusively for indices 0 through vl‑1.
RULE 100: When vl equals 0, the ordered sum reduction performs no vector‑element additions.
RULE 101: When vl equals 0, the scalar value in vs1[0] is copied unchanged to the destination register.
RULE 102: When vl equals 0, no NaN canonicalization, exception‑flag setting, or rounding is performed, preserving scalar‑summation handling of NaNs, exceptions, and rounding.
RULE 103: The active vector length register vl determines which elements of a vector register are active, and any element whose index is greater than or equal to vl is treated as masked‑off (not active).
RULE 104: In a reduction operation, if an input to a tree node is derived only from elements that are masked‑off or have indices greater than or equal to vl, the implementation may either treat that input as the additive identity for the appropriate EEW or copy the other input directly to the node’s output.
RULE 105: The reduction tree used by unordered‑sum reduction instructions must be deterministic for a given VTYPE configuration and the current value of vl.
RULE 106: For any vector mask‑logical instruction, all mask‑register elements whose indices are greater than or equal to the current value of vl are always written by the hardware.
RULE 107: The write to mask‑register elements with indices ≥ vl obeys a tail‑agnostic policy, meaning the architecture does not define a specific value to be stored.
RULE 108: The current value of vl defines the boundary between active elements (indices < vl) and tail elements (indices ≥ vl) for vector mask‑logical instructions.
RULE 109: Tail elements, i.e., mask‑register entries with indices ≥ vl, are not required to preserve their previous contents after a vector mask‑logical instruction.
RULE 110: The vector‑length register (vl) may hold the value zero.
RULE 111: When vl equals zero, the vcpop.m instruction writes the value 0 to its destination scalar register because no mask elements are active.
RULE 112: The architectural register vl is permitted to contain the value 0.
RULE 113: When vl equals 0, the vfirst.m instruction writes the destination integer register with the value –1 (all‑ones).
RULE 114: The vl CSR always contains the active vector length, defined as the number of vector elements fetched by the most‑recent vector load.
RULE 115: Executing a csrr instruction with vl as the source CSR copies the current value of vl into the specified general‑purpose register.
RULE 116: The vsetvli instruction updates the internal active vector length, and the updated value becomes the new content of the vl CSR.
RULE 117: When vsetvli writes its result to the zero register (x0), the vl CSR is still updated but no general‑purpose register receives the value.
RULE 118: When vsetvli writes its result to a non‑zero integer register, the computed active vector length is written both to that register and to the vl CSR.
RULE 119: After a vsetvli execution, any subsequent csrr …, vl instruction returns the active vector length that was computed by that vsetvli.
RULE 120: A csrr read of vl immediately after a vector load returns the number of bytes fetched by that load.
RULE 121: When a vid.v instruction executes, it writes to each element of the destination vector register group an index value ranging from 0 to vl‑1 inclusive.
RULE 122: The index of the last active element written by vid.v is exactly one less than the current value of the vl register.
RULE 123: The set of element indices generated by vid.v is defined by the current value of the vl register at the time the instruction is executed.
RULE 124: When the scalar‑to‑vector move instruction vmv.x.s is executed, it must read element 0 of the source vector register and write the result to the destination integer register regardless of the current vl value, even if vstart ≥ vl or vl = 0.
RULE 125: When the vector‑to‑scalar move instruction vmv.s.x is executed and vstart ≥ vl, it must perform no operation and must leave the destination vector register unchanged.
RULE 126: When vl = 0, the vector‑to‑scalar move instruction vmv.s.x must update zero elements of the destination vector register group, irrespective of the value of vstart.
RULE 127: The vector‑length register vl may contain the value zero.
RULE 128: When vl = 0, the vfmv.f.s instruction copies element 0 from the source vector register to the scalar floating‑point register.
RULE 129: When vl = 0, the vfmv.s.f instruction updates no elements of the destination vector register group.
RULE 130: The vfmv.f.s instruction executes its copy operation even if vstart ≥ vl.
RULE 131: The vfmv.s.f instruction performs no operation when vstart ≥ vl, leaving the destination vector register unchanged.
RULE 132: When vl = 0, vfmv.s.f updates no elements regardless of the value of vstart.
RULE 133: For any vslideup, vslidedown, vfslide1up, or vfslide1down instruction, if vstart is greater than or equal to vl then the instruction performs no operation and leaves the destination vector register unchanged.
RULE 134: When vl equals zero, no elements are updated in the destination vector register group for these slide instructions, regardless of the value of vstart.
RULE 135: The value in vl determines the maximum number of destination elements that a vslideup instruction may write.
RULE 136: Destination elements whose indices range from OFFSET through vl‑1 are written by vslideup only when they are unmasked and when OFFSET is less than vl.
RULE 137: For a vslideup instruction, the set of element indices that are processed is defined by 0 ≤ i < min(vl, max(vstart, OFFSET)).
RULE 138: In a vslideup instruction, element indices satisfying max(vstart, OFFSET) ≤ i < vl are left unchanged except for possible masking effects.
RULE 139: For a vslideup instruction, element indices from vl up to VLMAX‑1 follow the tail‑policy.
RULE 140: In a vslidedown instruction, the architectural register vl specifies the maximum number of destination vector elements that may be written.
RULE 141: For a vslidedown instruction, any element index i that is greater than or equal to vl is never written by the instruction; such elements are processed according to the current tail policy.
RULE 142: When vstart is less than vl, source elements are fetched for each slide index i such that 0 ≤ i + OFFSET < VLMAX, and if i + OFFSET ≥ VLMAX the source value is defined as zero.
RULE 143: When vstart is less than vl, destination elements with indices i satisfying 0 ≤ i < vstart remain unchanged.
RULE 144: When vstart is less than vl, destination elements with indices i satisfying vstart ≤ i < vl are written with the corresponding source value, provided the mask bit for that element is enabled.
RULE 145: When vstart is less than vl, destination elements with indices i satisfying vl ≤ i < VLMAX are handled according to the tail policy.
RULE 146: The vl register defines the maximum number of destination vector register elements that may be updated by any vector instruction; elements with indices greater than or equal to vl are not updated and are processed according to the current tail policy.
RULE 147: For the vslide1up instruction the value of vl limits participation to the first vl‑1 elements of the source vector register group, which are the only elements that can be copied to the destination vector register group.
RULE 148: When vl > 0 and an element index i is less than vstart, the vslide1up instruction leaves the destination element unchanged regardless of mask bits.
RULE 149: When vl > 0 and i equals vstart (or i equals 0 if vstart equals 0), the vslide1up instruction writes the scalar integer register x[rs1] to destination element vd[i] only if the corresponding mask bit v0.mask[i] is enabled.
RULE 150: When vl > 0 and i satisfies max(vstart, 1) ≤ i < vl, the vslide1up instruction writes the source element vs2[i‑1] to destination element vd[i] only if the corresponding mask bit v0.mask[i] is enabled.
RULE 151: For any element index i such that vl ≤ i < VLMAX, the vslide1up instruction does not update the destination element and the element is handled according to the current tail policy.
RULE 152: The vl register specifies the maximum number of destination vector‑register elements that may be written by a vector operation, and any element with an index greater than or equal to vl is not written but is instead processed according to the current tail policy.
RULE 153: For the vslide1down instruction, the scalar source register is written to the destination vector element at index vl‑1 only if that element is active.
RULE 154: If the element at index vl‑1 is not active for vslide1down, the update of that destination element follows the current mask‑agnostic or undisturbed policy.
RULE 155: In vslide1down, for any element index i satisfying vstart ≤ i < vl‑1, the destination element is written with the source vector element i+1 only when the corresponding mask bit v0.mask[i] is enabled.
RULE 156: In vslide1down, for element index i equal to vl‑1 and satisfying vstart ≤ i, the destination element is written with the scalar source register only when the corresponding mask bit v0.mask[i] is enabled.
RULE 157: In vslide1down, any destination element with an index i such that vl ≤ i < VLMAX is handled by the tail policy rather than being written with source values.
RULE 158: Because vl defines the highest active element index as vl‑1, any vector instruction that references vl‑1 must first determine whether that element is active, and if it is not active the mask policy governs whether a write occurs.
RULE 159: The value of the vl register specifies the maximum number of elements that a vector‑register‑gather instruction may write to its destination register.
RULE 160: For element indices that are greater than the current vl register value, processing of those elements follows the configured tail policy.
RULE 161: A source vector register group can be read at any element index less than VLMAX, and this read capability is independent of the current vl register value.
RULE 162: The vl register defines the count of vector register elements that are active for a given vector operation.
RULE 163: The vector mask register specified by vs1 selects elements only among the first vl elements of the source vector register group vs2.
RULE 164: Only the elements selected by the mask within the first vl elements are extracted and packed into the destination vector register group.
RULE 165: For vmv<nr>r.v instructions, vector elements are not written only when vstart ≥ evl, and the usual rule that no elements are written when vstart ≥ vl does not apply.
RULE 166: The vmv<nr>r.v instructions must function without requiring the compiler to read or modify the vl register.
RULE 167: When LMUL is greater than 1, the implementation must perform additional checks on the vl value because element groups may span multiple registers within a vector‑register group.
RULE 168: For element‑group sizes that are not powers of two, an element group may straddle individual vector registers inside a vector‑register group, so the vl setting must respect those boundaries.
RULE 169: Non‑power‑of‑two element‑group sizes can raise the lowest common multiple of element‑group sizes, thereby adding constraints on the allowed vl values.
RULE 170: The vl value must be selected so that no element group is divided across strip‑mine iterations in vector‑length‑agnostic code.
RULE 171: When a vector instruction’s source or destination operand is a vector of element groups, the architectural register vl must be set to an integer multiple of the element‑group size.
RULE 172: Any vl value that is not an integer multiple of the element‑group size for a vector‑of‑element‑group operand is reserved and therefore illegal.
RULE 173: If the application vector length (AVL) exceeds VLMAX, the value placed in vl must be either equal to VLMAX or a positive integer multiple of EGSMAX.
RULE 174: For the base vector extension where the element‑group size is 1, vl may be any integer value up to VLMAX.
RULE 175: vsetvl instructions that set vl must select a SEW that is consistent with the encoded element width (EEW) of the instruction or with the EEW of all vector‑of‑element‑group operands.
RULE 176: When an instruction has multiple vector‑of‑element‑group operands with differing EEW, the implementation must choose a SEW that satisfies all operands before setting vl.
RULE 177: Implementation‑specific constraints may further restrict the set of legal vl values for particular element‑group instructions to guarantee that all operands receive legal length values.
RULE 178: The vector length register vl determines the number of elements that a vector instruction will process.
RULE 179: For vector crypto instructions, the number of element groups operated on is computed as vl divided by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 180: For any Vector Crypto instruction that operates on element groups, vl must be an integer multiple of the instruction’s Element Group Size (EGS).
RULE 181: If vl is not a multiple of the required EGS for a Vector Crypto instruction, the instruction is reserved and its behavior is architecturally undefined, possibly raising a reserved‑instruction exception.
RULE 182: For all vaes* Vector Crypto instructions, the required EGS for vl is 4.
RULE 183: For all vsha2* Vector Crypto instructions, the required EGS for vl is 4.
RULE 184: For all vg* Vector Crypto instructions, the required EGS for vl is 4.
RULE 185: For all vsm3* Vector Crypto instructions, the required EGS for vl is 8.
RULE 186: For all vsm4* Vector Crypto instructions, the required EGS for vl is 4.
RULE 187: For each listed Vector Crypto instruction, vl modulo EGS must equal zero; otherwise the instruction is reserved.
RULE 188: The multiple‑of‑EGS requirement that applies to vl also applies to vstart.
RULE 189: The architectural vl register determines the number of element groups that are processed by any vector instruction.
RULE 190: Required LMUL values for certain algorithms are expressed as fractions of vl.
RULE 191: For the SHA‑512 algorithm with VLEN = 64, the required LMUL value equals vl divided by 2.
RULE 192: For the SM3 algorithm with VLEN = 32, the required LMUL value equals vl divided by 4.
RULE 193: The number of element groups to be processed is computed as vl divided by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 194: vl must be programmed to equal the total number of 32‑bit elements (SEW = 32) that are to be processed.
RULE 195: vl must always be a multiple of the element‑group size, which is 4.
RULE 196: For AES vector instructions, the hardware determines the number of element groups processed by dividing the current value of vl by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 197: For AES vector instructions, software must set vl to the exact number of SEW‑32 elements that are to be processed.
RULE 198: For AES vector instructions, the value written to vl must be an integer multiple of the element‑group size, which is 4.
RULE 199: The hardware computes the number of element groups to be processed as the integer division of vl by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 200: vl must be set to the total number of SEW elements to be processed and therefore must be an integer multiple of the element‑group size, which is defined as 4.
RULE 201: The number of element groups processed is calculated as vl divided by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 202: When the selected element width (SEW) is 32 bits, vl must be set to the number of 32‑bit elements to be processed.
RULE 203: vl must be an integer multiple of the element‑group size (EGS), which is 4.
RULE 204: The number of element groups to be processed is computed as vl divided by EGS.
RULE 205: vl must be set to the number of elements whose SEW is 32 that are to be processed.
RULE 206: vl must be a multiple of EGS, which is 8.
RULE 207: For Zvkt instructions, the vl register is considered a control value and not a data operand with respect to Data-Independent Execution Latency.
RULE 208: The value in the vl register is exempt from the latency-independence guarantees of Data-Independent Execution Latency that apply to all data operands of Zvkt instructions.
RULE 209: For the vaesdf instruction, the vector‑length register vl is divided by the element‑group size (EGS) to produce eg_len, the total number of element groups to be processed.
RULE 210: For the vaesdf instruction, the element‑group size (EGS) used in the division of vl is a constant value of 4.
RULE 211: The upper bound of the element‑group processing loop for vaesdf is the eg_len value that results from dividing vl by EGS, so the loop never iterates with an index equal to or exceeding eg_len.
RULE 212: The range of element groups that the vaesdf instruction operates on is limited by vl, such that the highest element‑group index processed is (vl / EGS) − 1.
RULE 213: The current value of vl is used by the instruction to determine how many element groups will be processed.
RULE 214: The number of element groups processed (eg_len) is calculated as the integer division of vl by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 215: The integer‑division result of vl divided by EGS specifies the count of element groups of the vector register that the instruction operates on.
RULE 216: The architectural register vl contains the current vector length used by vector operations.
RULE 217: For a vaesef instruction, the number of element groups to be processed (eg_len) is calculated as the integer division of vl by the element‑group size EGS.
RULE 218: In a vaesef instruction, vl influences the range of element groups iterated over by contributing to the computation of eg_len.
RULE 219: The vaesem instruction uses the current value of the vl register to compute how many element groups are processed.
RULE 220: The number of element groups (eg_len) is calculated as the integer division of the vl register by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 221: The vl register value determines the count of element groups iterated over during the execution of the vaesem instruction.
RULE 222: The effective group length for a vector instruction is computed as the integer division of the current value of the vl register by the element grouping size (EGS), i.e., eg_len = (vl / EGS).
RULE 223: The start index of the effective group is obtained by integer‑dividing the VSTART register by EGS, and the VSTART value used in this calculation is derived from, or constrained by, the current vl register value.
RULE 224: The architectural register vl holds the current vector length that determines how many element groups the vaeskf2.vi instruction may process.
RULE 225: For the vaeskf2.vi instruction the number of element groups to be processed, eg_len, is calculated as the integer division of vl by the element‑group size EGS.
RULE 226: The value eg_len derived from vl establishes the exclusive upper bound for the element‑group loop, so the loop iterates while the group index i is less than eg_len.
RULE 227: The element‑group loop for vaeskf2.vi iterates with index i from the starting group up to eg_len‑1, where eg_len is defined as vl divided by EGS.
RULE 228: The architectural vl register value is divided by the element‑group size (EGS) using integer division that truncates toward zero to compute eg_len, the total number of element groups the instruction will process.
RULE 229: The instruction iterates over element groups i from eg_start up to eg_len‑1, where the upper bound eg_len‑1 is derived from the value of vl.
RULE 230: The number of element groups processed by the instruction is exactly the integer quotient of vl divided by EGS; no additional groups are processed beyond this count.
RULE 231: The vl register is read at the start of the instruction to determine eg_len and its value remains unchanged by the execution of the instruction.
RULE 232: A vector instruction processes elements only for indices i that satisfy i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 233: The element whose index equals vl‑1 is included in the set of elements processed by the instruction.
RULE 234: The iteration range for a vector instruction is defined by the condition vstart ≤ i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 235: The vl register defines the exclusive upper bound for element indices that a vector instruction may process.
RULE 236: For any vector instruction, only elements with index i satisfying vstart ≤ i < vl are operated on.
RULE 237: The vl register defines the upper bound of the element index range for vector instructions, making the highest element index that can be processed equal to vl‑1.
RULE 238: During execution of a vector instruction that iterates over element indices, the hardware starts the iteration at the current vstart value and continues consecutively until the index reaches vl‑1 inclusive.
RULE 239: The value contained in the vl register is consulted for every vector instruction to determine whether an element index is within the permissible processing range.
RULE 240: Updating the vl register changes the maximum element index that subsequent vector instructions are allowed to process.
RULE 241: A vector instruction must not process any element with an index greater than vl‑1, regardless of other register values.
RULE 242: For any vector instruction, the element index variable i starts at vstart and increments while i ≤ vl‑1, processing all indices i such that vstart ≤ i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 243: The value stored in the vl register defines the highest element index that the instruction will operate on, which is always vl‑1.
RULE 244: When the vl register holds zero, the range vstart … vl‑1 is empty and the vector instruction performs no element‑wise operations.
RULE 245: For the vclz.v instruction, vector elements are processed only when their indices satisfy vstart ≤ i ≤ vl‑1, meaning the operation applies exclusively to elements whose index is less than the current value of the vl register.
RULE 246: The loop bound used by vclz.v is vl‑1, establishing that the highest element index that can be accessed by the instruction is one less than the value held in the vl register.
RULE 247: The architectural register vl specifies the maximum index of vector elements that are considered active for a vector instruction, establishing the upper bound of the active element range.
RULE 248: For instructions such as vcpop.v, the processing loop iterates over element indices i starting at vstart and continuing up to vl‑1 inclusive, thereby using vl to determine the final element index processed.
RULE 249: An element index i is considered active for the instruction only if i is less than vl, because the loop terminates at vl‑1.
RULE 250: In the vctz.v algorithm, the iteration variable i runs from vstart up to vl‑1, using the current value of the vl register as the upper bound.
RULE 251: The vctz.v instruction processes exactly those vector elements whose indices are strictly less than the current vl value.
RULE 252: The vghsh.vv instruction uses the current value of the vector‑length register vl to decide how many element groups are operated on.
RULE 253: For the vghsh.vv instruction the element‑group size is 4, and it computes eg_len as the integer division of vl by 4.
RULE 254: The computed eg_len is used as the loop’s upper bound, causing the vghsh.vv instruction to process exactly vl divided by 4 element groups.
RULE 255: The architectural register vl holds the current vector length.
RULE 256: During execution of the vgmul.vv instruction, the value of vl is divided by the instruction’s element‑group size (EGS) to produce the number of element groups, calculated as eg_len = vl / EGS.
RULE 257: The architectural register vl always contains the current vector length for the executing vector instruction.
RULE 258: The value of vl defines the exclusive upper bound for element indices, preventing any index equal to or greater than vl from being processed.
RULE 259: Vector element processing is performed for every index i that satisfies vstart ≤ i ≤ vl‑1, where vl is the current vector length.
RULE 260: The VROL operation iterates element indices beginning at vstart and terminates when the index reaches the current value of the vl register, so it never processes an index equal to or greater than vl.
RULE 261: The VROL operation processes each element only while its index i satisfies i ≤ vl‑1, meaning the element index must be strictly less than the current vl value.
RULE 262: The vl register holds the current vector length.
RULE 263: For vror instructions, the operation is performed for each element index i ranging from vstart to vl − 1.
RULE 264: The value of vl determines the highest element index that can be processed, so no element with an index equal to or greater than vl is processed.
RULE 265: The architectural register vl always contains the current vector length measured in number of elements.
RULE 266: For any vector operation that defines an element‑group size EGS, the effective group length eg_len is computed as the integer division of vl by EGS (eg_len = vl / EGS, truncating toward zero).
RULE 267: The number of loop iterations performed by the VSHA2c instruction is determined by vl because the loop runs from eg_start up to eg_len‑1, and eg_len is derived from vl.
RULE 268: The vl CSR holds the current vector length value used by the hardware to control vector instruction execution.
RULE 269: Software may program the vl register with a specific integer (for example 4) to specify the exact number of vector elements that a vector-merge instruction will process.
RULE 270: For each vector instruction the hardware computes eg_len as the integer division of vl by the element-group size (EGS), thereby determining how many element groups the instruction will iterate over.
RULE 271: The architectural behavior of vl is to serve as the controller of the total number of element groups processed by a vector instruction.
RULE 272: The architectural vector‑length register vl holds the current vector length value that vector instructions use to determine processing size.
RULE 273: For a vector instruction, the number of element groups processed (eg_len) is computed as the integer division of vl by the instruction’s effective group size (EGS).
RULE 274: The vl register holds the current vector length that defines how many vector elements are active for the executing instruction.
RULE 275: For the vsm3me.vv instruction, the implementation computes eg_len as the integer division of the vl register value by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 276: The integer division of vl by EGS determines the number of element groups that the vsm3me.vv instruction processes, with a loop iterating from eg_start up to eg_len‑1.
RULE 277: For the vsm4k.vi instruction the architectural value of vl is divided by the element‑group size (EGS = 4) using integer division to compute eg_len, the effective number of element groups the instruction will process.
RULE 278: The integer‑division result eg_len derived from vl establishes the exclusive upper bound of the iteration loop, so the instruction processes element groups with indices i such that i < eg_len.
RULE 279: Because eg_len is computed directly from vl, any change to vl before the execution of vsm4k.vi changes the number of element groups that the instruction operates on.
RULE 280: The element‑group size employed in the division of vl for vsm4k.vi is fixed at four scalar elements per group.
RULE 281: The execution of vsm4k.vi does not modify the vl register; vl solely determines the count of groups processed.
RULE 282: For the vsm4r instruction, the vector‑length register vl provides the operand that determines how many element groups the instruction will process.
RULE 283: The element‑group length used by vsm4r is calculated as the integer division of vl by the element‑group size EGS.
RULE 284: The value obtained from dividing vl by EGS is the effective length in element groups (eg_len) for the vsm4r instruction’s loop.
RULE 285: The vl register defines the exclusive upper bound of active vector element indices, so a vector instruction executes only for element indices i that satisfy i < vl.
RULE 286: For any vector instruction, the active element range is all indices i that satisfy vstart ≤ i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 287: The vl register is a vector‑extension CSR whose state is tracked by the VS field in the mstatus register.
RULE 288: The VS field encodes the status of the vector extension state, and this encoding includes the vl CSR.
RULE 289: If the VS field in mstatus is set to Off, any instruction that attempts to read or write the vl CSR must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 290: Changing the VS field to any value (Off, Initial, Clean, or Dirty) does not modify the stored value of the vl register.
RULE 291: In M-mode, accessing vl register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0xc20; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 292: In S-mode, accessing vl register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0xc20; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 293: In U-mode, accessing vl register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0xc20; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 294: In M-mode, setting vl.VALUE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0xc20, a0; csrr a1, 0xc20; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 295: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the vl register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0xc20, a0; csrr a1, 0xc20; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 296: In M-mode, accessing vl register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0xc20; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 297: In M-mode, accessing vl register while Zvkt extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zvkt; csrr a0, 0xc20; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: vlenb
NUMBER: 0xc22
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: MXLEN
FIELDS: 63..0=VALUE
EXTENSIONS: V
RULE 1: vlenb is one of seven unprivileged CSRs defined by the RISC‑V vector extension.
RULE 2: The architectural address of vlenb is 0xC22.
RULE 3: vlenb has privilege level URO, indicating it is unprivileged and read‑only.
RULE 4: The architectural value of vlenb equals VLEN divided by 8, representing the vector register length in bytes.
RULE 5: vlenb always holds the constant value VLEN/8 and cannot be altered by software.
RULE 6: Any attempt to write vlenb must be ignored or raise an illegal‑instruction trap in accordance with the URO access type.
RULE 7: vlenb is a read‑only control‑and‑status register (CSR).
RULE 8: vlenb is XLEN bits wide, where XLEN matches the implementation’s integer register width (e.g., 32 or 64 bits).
RULE 9: vlenb holds the value VLEN divided by 8, representing the vector register length in bytes.
RULE 10: The value contained in vlenb is a design‑time constant that does not change during program execution.
RULE 11: For a given hardware implementation, vlenb provides a fixed value that remains unchanged at runtime.
RULE 12: The existence of vlenb enables software to obtain the vector length in bytes directly, avoiding the need to compute it from other registers and preserving current vl and vtype settings.
RULE 13: The vlenb register reports the number of bytes transferred by each whole‑register vector load or store instruction.
RULE 14: Software obtains the current byte‑transfer count for whole‑register vector operations by reading the vlenb register.
RULE 15: vlenb is a control‑status register that is defined by the RISC‑V Vector (V) extension.
RULE 16: The vector‑extension architectural state includes the vector registers v0‑v31 and the CSRs vcsr, vxrm, vxsat, vstart, vl, vtype, and vlenb.
RULE 17: Any write to the vlenb CSR is a modification of vector‑extension state and participates in the VS status tracking defined for the VS field in the mstatus register.
RULE 18: A write to vlenb may cause the VS field in mstatus to transition from Initial or Clean to Dirty in accordance with the VS state‑transition rules.
RULE 19: In M-mode, accessing vlenb register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0xc22; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 20: In S-mode, accessing vlenb register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0xc22; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 21: In U-mode, accessing vlenb register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0xc22; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 22: In M-mode, setting vlenb.VALUE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0xc22, a0; csrr a1, 0xc22; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 23: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the vlenb register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0xc22, a0; csrr a1, 0xc22; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 24: In M-mode, accessing vlenb register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0xc22; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: vstart
NUMBER: 0x008
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: MXLEN
FIELDS: 63..0=VALUE
EXTENSIONS: V, Zvkg
RULE 1: The architectural control‑and‑status register vstart is located at CSR address 0x008.
RULE 2: The CSR vstart is accessible at user privilege level for both reads and writes (URW).
RULE 3: The vstart register holds the vector start element index that determines where a vector instruction begins execution.
RULE 4: The value read from vstart specifies the index of the first element to be processed by the currently executing vector instruction.
RULE 5: vstart is an XLEN‑bit read‑write CSR that holds the index of the first element to be executed by a vector instruction.
RULE 6: When a trap occurs during execution of a vector instruction, hardware writes vstart with the element index at which the trap occurred.
RULE 7: After a trap is handled, execution of the trapped vector instruction resumes at the element index stored in vstart.
RULE 8: Each vector instruction starts execution using the element number currently held in vstart.
RULE 9: Elements whose indices are lower than the value in vstart are left unchanged in the destination vector of a vector instruction.
RULE 10: At the completion of any vector instruction, vstart is reset to zero.
RULE 11: The reset‑to‑zero behavior also applies to the vector‑setting instructions vsetvl and vsetvli.
RULE 12: Vector instructions that raise illegal‑instruction exceptions do not modify vstart.
RULE 13: Implementations may raise illegal‑instruction exceptions when a vector instruction is executed with a vstart value that cannot be generated for the current vtype setting.
RULE 14: Implementations may raise illegal‑instruction exceptions when certain vector arithmetic instructions are executed with a non‑zero vstart on implementations that never take interrupts during those instructions.
RULE 15: The vstart CSR provides only the number of writable bits required to represent the largest valid element index (one less than the maximum VLMAX).
RULE 16: For the maximum configuration (LMUL = 8, SEW = 8) with VLEN = 256, vstart requires 8 writable bits to represent indices 0 through 255.
RULE 17: Values of vstart that exceed the largest element index permitted by the current vtype setting are reserved.
RULE 18: Implementations may trap on out‑of‑range vstart values but are not required to.
RULE 19: Unprivileged code may write to vstart.
RULE 20: Writing a non‑zero value to vstart may have performance implications on some implementations.
RULE 21: vstart is implemented as a Control and Status Register (CSR) in the RISC‑V vector extension.
RULE 22: After a processor reset, vstart may contain any value and the architecture does not require it to be defined.
RULE 23: Executing an initial vsetvl instruction must set vstart to a defined state before any further vector‑unit operations.
RULE 24: Executing an initial vsetivl instruction must set vstart to a defined state before any further vector‑unit operations.
RULE 25: The defined state to which vstart is set by an initial vsetvl or vsetivl instruction is normally zero.
RULE 26: When vstart is non‑zero, a masked vector instruction must not have its destination vector register group overlap the source mask register v0 unless the destination is being written with a mask value.
RULE 27: When vstart is non‑zero, a masked vector instruction must not have its destination vector register group overlap the source mask register v0 unless the destination is being written with a scalar reduction result.
RULE 28: The restriction that a masked vector instruction’s destination vector register group may not overlap source mask register v0 (except for mask writes or scalar reduction results) exists to enable correct restart behavior with a non‑zero vstart.
RULE 29: Prestart elements are those vector elements whose index x satisfies 0 ≤ x < vstart.
RULE 30: Prestart elements never raise exceptions.
RULE 31: Prestart elements never update any destination vector register group.
RULE 32: Body elements are those vector elements whose index x satisfies vstart ≤ x < vl.
RULE 33: The definition of the body element set depends solely on the current vstart value.
RULE 34: If vstart ≥ vl then there are no body elements.
RULE 35: If vstart ≥ vl then no elements are updated in any destination vector register group.
RULE 36: If vstart ≥ vl then tail elements are not updated with agnostic values; the vtype.vta agnostic‑update behavior does not apply.
RULE 37: If vl = 0 then no elements, including any agnostic elements, are updated in the destination vector register group regardless of vstart.
RULE 38: The initial vstart value is read at the start of each vector instruction and is used to partition element indices into prestart, body, and tail subsets.
RULE 39: The architectural vstart register may hold a non‑zero value when a vector load or store instruction begins execution.
RULE 40: A vector load or store instruction may accept a non‑zero vstart value as part of its operation.
RULE 41: A vector load or store instruction may update (generate) the architectural vstart register with a new non‑zero value during its execution.
RULE 42: For the vector unit‑stride mask load (vlm.v) and mask store (vsm.v) instructions, vstart is interpreted in units of bytes.
RULE 43: For the vector unit‑stride mask load (vlm.v) and mask store (vsm.v) instructions, vstart specifies the byte offset applied to the memory address used by these byte‑oriented operations.
RULE 44: If vstart = 0 and vl > 0, the implementation must process at least one element before it may reduce vl.
RULE 45: When vstart is non‑zero, the implementation may process fewer than the remaining vl elements and may consequently reduce vl.
RULE 46: When a fault‑only‑first vector load instruction traps because of an interrupt, the implementation must write vstart with the element index at which execution should resume after the interrupt.
RULE 47: When a fault‑only‑first vector load instruction traps because of an interrupt, the implementation must not reduce vl while updating vstart to the appropriate resume value.
RULE 48: The vstart register holds a value that is interpreted in units of whole segments for vector segment load and store instructions.
RULE 49: The vstart register is used to indicate the current segment index during the execution of a segment‑wise memory operation.
RULE 50: When a trap occurs while accessing a segment, the architecture does not prescribe whether any memory accesses belonging to that segment associated with the current vstart value are completed before the trap, and this outcome is implementation‑defined.
RULE 51: For vector whole‑register load and store instructions, the architectural rule that suppresses element writes when vstart ≥ vl does not apply.
RULE 52: For vector whole‑register load and store instructions, element writes are suppressed when vstart ≥ evl, where evl is defined as NFIELDS × VLEN / EEW.
RULE 53: If a vector‑floating‑point instruction is executed while the vstart register contains a value greater than or equal to the current vector length (vl), then the behavior of that instruction is reserved.
RULE 54: For a vector integer merge instruction, the architectural definition of the "body" elements is the set of vector elements whose indices satisfy vstart ≤ i < vl.
