RULE 55: A vector integer merge instruction performs element‑wise processing only for indices i that are greater than or equal to vstart.
RULE 56: Elements with indices i less than vstart are never processed by a vector integer merge instruction.
RULE 57: The vector‑scalar floating‑point merge instruction begins processing body elements at the element index specified by vstart.
RULE 58: The vector‑scalar floating‑point merge instruction processes every body element whose index is greater than or equal to vstart and less than the current vector length vl, ignoring any mask value.
RULE 59: When a trap is taken on a vector reduction instruction, the architectural vstart register is reported as zero.
RULE 60: Executing a vector reduction instruction while vstart is non‑zero must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 61: For any vector‑mask logical instruction, the architectural effect on each element whose index is less than the current value of vstart is that the element remains unchanged.
RULE 62: For any vector‑mask logical instruction, the architecture resets vstart to zero after the instruction has completed execution.
RULE 63: The treatment of vstart for vector‑mask logical instructions is defined to be identical to its treatment for all other vector instructions.
RULE 64: When a trap is generated by the vcpop.m instruction, the architectural vstart register is reported as zero.
RULE 65: The vcpop.m instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception if the architectural vstart register contains a non‑zero value.
RULE 66: When a trap occurs on a vfirst instruction, the architectural state must report the vstart register value as zero.
RULE 67: The vfirst instruction must raise an illegal-instruction exception if the vstart register is non-zero at the time the instruction is executed.
RULE 68: When a trap is taken on execution of the vmsbf.m instruction, the architectural state must report vstart equal to 0.
RULE 69: The vmsbf.m instruction raises an illegal‑instruction exception whenever the architectural value of vstart is non‑zero.
RULE 70: When a trap occurs during the execution of the vmsif.m instruction, the architectural state reports the vstart register as 0.
RULE 71: The vmsif.m instruction generates an illegal‑instruction exception if vstart is not zero.
RULE 72: When a trap is caused by the vmsof.m instruction, the architectural state reports vstart equal to 0.
RULE 73: The vmsof.m instruction raises an illegal-instruction exception if the vstart CSR holds a non-zero value at the time the instruction is executed.
RULE 74: When a trap occurs during execution of a viota.m instruction, the architectural register vstart is set to 0.
RULE 75: After handling a trap taken on a viota.m instruction, the processor restarts the viota.m instruction with vstart equal to 0.
RULE 76: If the architectural register vstart is non‑zero when a viota.m instruction is decoded, an illegal‑instruction exception is raised.
RULE 77: For the vmv.x.s instruction, the operation is performed even when vstart ≥ vl or vl = 0, so the element at index zero of the source vector is read and written to the integer register irrespective of the current vstart value.
RULE 78: For the vmv.s.x instruction, if vstart ≥ vl, the instruction performs no operation and does not modify the destination register.
RULE 79: For the vmv.s.x instruction, when vl = 0, no elements of the destination vector register group are updated, regardless of the value of vstart.
RULE 80: The vfmv.f.s instruction must execute its operation even when vstart is greater than or equal to the current vector length (vl) or when vl equals zero.
RULE 81: When vstart is greater than or equal to vl, the vfmv.f.s instruction copies element 0 of the source vector register into the destination floating‑point scalar register.
RULE 82: The vfmv.s.f instruction must not perform any operation and must leave its destination register unmodified if vstart is greater than or equal to vl.
RULE 83: When vstart is greater than or equal to vl, the vfmv.s.f instruction results in no update to element 0 of the destination vector register.
RULE 84: For any vslideup, vslidedown, vfslide1up, vfslide1down, vfsld1down, or related slide instruction, if the current value of vstart is greater than or equal to vl, the instruction performs no operation and leaves the destination vector register unchanged.
RULE 85: When vl equals zero, no elements of the destination vector register group are updated by these slide instructions, regardless of the value of vstart.
RULE 86: A vslideup instruction only defines destination elements for which the current value of vstart is less than the vector length vl.
RULE 87: The set of indices written by a vslideup instruction is 0 ≤ i < min(vl, max(vstart, OFFSET)), where OFFSET is the slide‑up amount.
RULE 88: For a vslideup instruction, all destination elements with indices i that satisfy max(vstart, OFFSET) ≤ i < vl must remain unchanged.
RULE 89: Destination elements with indices i such that vl ≤ i < VLMAX are processed according to the tail‑policy, independent of the value of vstart.
RULE 90: The architecture permits a non‑zero value in vstart to be used for restarting a vslideup instruction after a trap, provided the source and destination register groups do not overlap.
RULE 91: The vstart register specifies the start index for vector slide‑down operations.
RULE 92: The vstart register is consulted in the per‑element behavior rules for both source element selection and destination element handling.
RULE 93: For a destination element i, the source element is read from vs2[i+OFFSET] only if vstart < vl and 0 <= i+OFFSET < VLMAX.
RULE 94: If i+OFFSET >= VLMAX, the source element is defined as zero irrespective of the value of vstart.
RULE 95: For any element index i satisfying 0 <= i < vstart, the destination element vd[i] remains unchanged.
RULE 96: For any element index i satisfying vstart <= i < vl, the destination element vd[i] is written with the source value when the corresponding mask bit v0.mask[i] is enabled.
RULE 97: For any element index i satisfying vl <= i < VLMAX, the destination element vd[i] is processed according to the current tail‑policy, independent of vstart.
RULE 98: The condition vstart < vl determines whether the slide‑down operation is active for the current vector length.
RULE 99: When vstart >= vl, no source elements are read and all destination elements are handled by the tail‑policy.
RULE 100: The architectural register vstart acts as an index threshold that determines which elements of a vector instruction may be updated.
RULE 101: For the vslide1up instruction when vl > 0, any element whose index i is less than vstart is left unchanged regardless of the mask setting.
RULE 102: For the vslide1up instruction when vl > 0, the element whose index i equals vstart is written with the scalar integer value from integer register rs1 (vd[i] = x[rs1]) only if the corresponding mask bit v0.mask[i] is enabled; if the mask bit is disabled the element follows the mask‑agnostic undisturbed policy.
RULE 103: For the vslide1up instruction when vl > 0, every element whose index i satisfies max(vstart,1) ≤ i < vl is written with the value from the source vector register group shifted by one element (vd[i] = vs2[i‑1]) only if the corresponding mask bit v0.mask[i] is enabled; if the mask bit is disabled the element remains unchanged according to the mask‑agnostic policy.
RULE 104: The vstart‑dependent behavior for vslide1up is defined only for indices i < vl; elements with index i ≥ vl are processed according to the current tail policy and are not affected by vstart.
RULE 105: For any vector instruction, all element indices i such that i < vstart are left unchanged by the instruction.
RULE 106: For any vector instruction, all element indices i that satisfy vstart ≤ i < vl‑1 are processed according to the instruction’s normal semantics and any applicable mask.
RULE 107: For any vector instruction, the element with index i = vl‑1, when active, is processed according to the instruction’s normal semantics and this processing occurs even when vstart ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 108: For any vector instruction, element indices i that satisfy vl ≤ i < VLMAX are handled according to the current tail policy, and this handling is independent of the value in vstart.
RULE 109: The architectural effect of the vstart register is to establish the lower bound on element indices that a vector instruction may modify.
RULE 110: Elements whose indices are below the value in vstart are exempt from modification by any vector instruction, regardless of masking.
RULE 111: Elements whose indices are at or above vstart and below the active vector length are treated as active and follow the instruction’s normal semantics and masking rules.
RULE 112: When a trap is taken on a vcompress instruction, the architectural vstart CSR reported to the trap handler is zero.
RULE 113: If the architectural vstart CSR holds a non‑zero value when a vcompress instruction is executed, the implementation must raise an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 114: When a vcompress instruction is restarted after a trap, the implementation shall restart processing from element index zero regardless of the prior value of vstart.
RULE 115: After a vcompress instruction is restarted from element zero, elements of the destination register that would have been written for indices greater than the original non‑zero vstart may already contain updated values.
RULE 116: For vmv<nr>r.v whole‑vector‑register‑move instructions, the instruction writes no vector elements when the current vstart value is greater than or equal to evl.
RULE 117: For vmv<nr>r.v whole‑vector‑register‑move instructions, the condition vstart ≥ vl does not prevent element writes; element writes are instead suppressed only when vstart ≥ evl.
RULE 118: For vmv<nr>r.v whole‑vector‑register‑move instructions, vstart is compared against evl, where evl is calculated as EMUL multiplied by VLEN divided by SEW.
RULE 119: The hardware writes the vstart CSR whenever a trap—whether a synchronous exception or an asynchronous interrupt—occurs during the execution of a vector instruction.
RULE 120: After such a trap, the vstart CSR contains the element index of the vector instruction at which the trap was taken.
RULE 121: The implementation may resume a partially‑executed vector instruction after trap handling by starting execution at the element index stored in vstart, thereby reducing interrupt latency and simplifying forward‑progress guarantees.
RULE 122: vstart is a CSR that holds the element index to be used when a vector instruction traps.
RULE 123: For any trapping vector instruction, all operations that affect result elements with indices less than vstart must have already committed their results to architectural state.
RULE 124: For any trapping vector instruction, operations that affect result elements with indices equal to or greater than vstart must not have altered architectural state, except when restarting the instruction from vstart would still produce the correct final state.
RULE 125: Implementations may permit updates to elements with indices greater than vstart at trap time provided that re‑executing the instruction starting at vstart overwrites those elements correctly.
RULE 126: When a vector store executes in an idempotent memory region, it may update elements whose indices are beyond the element that caused a synchronous trap.
RULE 127: When a vector store executes in a non‑idempotent memory region, it must not update any elements whose indices are equal to or greater than the element that caused the trap.
RULE 128: The restart point for a trapped vector instruction shall be the element index specified by vstart.
RULE 129: Implementations must ensure forward progress can eventually be achieved for the element or segment whose index is indicated by vstart.
RULE 130: Imprecise traps caused by synchronous vector exceptions shall set the vstart register to the index of the faulting element.
RULE 131: The starting element group for a vector‑crypto instruction is calculated as the integer division of the architectural register vstart by the element‑group size (EGS) defined for that instruction.
RULE 132: The architectural register vstart holds the vector‑start index used by vector‑crypto instructions.
RULE 133: The value stored in vstart must satisfy the constraints on allowed vstart values defined for vector‑crypto instructions.
RULE 134: The vstart register holds a count of vector elements and is interpreted as the element index from which a vector instruction begins processing.
RULE 135: For any Vector Crypto instruction that operates on element groups, the vstart value must be an integer multiple of that instruction’s Element Group Size (EGS).
RULE 136: If the vstart value is not an integer multiple of the required EGS for the executing Vector Crypto instruction, the instruction is reserved, its behavior is undefined, and it must not be implemented.
RULE 137: For all vaes* Vector Crypto instructions, the vstart value must be a multiple of 4.
RULE 138: For all vsha2* Vector Crypto instructions, the vstart value must be a multiple of 4.
RULE 139: For all vg* Vector Crypto instructions, the vstart value must be a multiple of 4.
RULE 140: For all vsm4* Vector Crypto instructions, the vstart value must be a multiple of 4.
RULE 141: For all vsm3* Vector Crypto instructions, the vstart value must be a multiple of 8.
RULE 142: When executing Zvkg vector GCM/GMAC instructions, the architectural vstart register must contain a value that is a multiple of the element‑group size (EGS) equal to 4.
RULE 143: vstart must be a multiple of the element-group size (EGS).
RULE 144: For these AES vector instructions, the element-group size (EGS) is 4, therefore vstart must be a multiple of 4.
RULE 145: The architectural vstart register value shall always be a multiple of the element‑group size, which is 4.
RULE 146: When configuring the vector unit for SHA‑2 instructions, the architectural vstart register value shall be aligned to a 4‑element boundary.
RULE 147: When executing SM4 vector instructions, the vstart register must hold a value that is a multiple of 4.
RULE 148: When any SM3 vector instruction is executed, the value stored in the vstart CSR must be a multiple of that instruction's element‑group size.
RULE 149: For all SM3 vector instructions the element‑group size is defined as 8, so the vstart CSR value must be a multiple of 8.
RULE 150: The vstart register is read by the execution model of each vector instruction to determine the starting element‑group index.
RULE 151: The starting element‑group index is calculated as the integer division of vstart by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 152: A vector instruction iterates over element‑group indices i from the value computed from vstart (vstart / EGS) up to eg_len‑1.
RULE 153: The value eg_len is defined as VL divided by EGS and is not influenced by the vstart register.
RULE 154: The first element group processed by a vector instruction is the group whose index equals vstart divided by EGS.
RULE 155: For a vector‑AES instruction, the hardware uses the current value of the vstart CSR to decide the element‑group index at which the instruction begins its operation.
RULE 156: The vector‑AES instruction computes the starting element‑group index as the integer result of dividing the vstart value by the instruction’s element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 157: The vector‑AES instruction iterates over element groups starting with the group index given by vstart/EGS and continues sequentially up to the end‑group index (eg_len‑1).
RULE 158: Architecturally, the vstart CSR is interpreted as a count of individual elements, and its value must be divided by the element‑group size to obtain the first element group processed by a vector‑AES instruction.
RULE 159: The instruction reads the architectural register vstart.
RULE 160: The instruction computes eg_start as the integer quotient of vstart divided by the element‑group size (EGS) for that instruction.
RULE 161: The value of vstart, after being divided to form eg_start, is used as the lower bound of the element‑group loop that iterates from eg_start to eg_len‑1.
RULE 162: The value stored in vstart determines the index of the first element group the instruction will operate on after a trap or any other restart condition.
RULE 163: The architectural start index for element‑group processing of the vaesem instruction is taken from the vstart CSR.
RULE 164: The element‑group start value eg_start is calculated as the integer division of vstart by the element‑group size EGS.
RULE 165: For AES‑128 vaesem operations the element‑group size EGS is fixed to 4, so eg_start equals vstart divided by 4.
RULE 166: The value eg_start derived from vstart is used as the lower bound of the loop that iterates over element groups in the vaesem implementation.
RULE 167: Consequently, the vstart register provides the element‑group offset at which execution of a vector instruction such as vaesem begins.
RULE 168: In the VAESKF1 instruction, the starting element‑group index (eg_start) is computed as the integer division of the architectural register vstart by the element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 169: The VAESKF1 instruction processes element groups beginning at the index derived from vstart and continues sequentially up to the index (vl/EGS)‑1.
RULE 170: The value held in vstart directly selects the subset of element groups on which VAESKF1 operates.
RULE 171: The vstart register supplies the vector start index that determines where a vector instruction begins processing elements.
RULE 172: The effective‑group start index (eg_start) is computed by dividing the value in vstart by the element‑group size (EGS) using integer division.
RULE 173: The vstart‑derived eg_start is used as the lower bound of the element‑group loop, iterating i from that value through eg_len‑1.
RULE 174: If the value of vstart is not a multiple of the element‑group size EGS (i.e., vstart % EGS ≠ 0) the instruction must raise an illegal‑instruction exception and retire as a failed instruction.
RULE 175: When vstart is a multiple of EGS, the implementation must compute the starting element‑group index as eg_start = vstart / EGS.
RULE 176: The instruction must iterate over element groups i starting from the index computed from vstart (vstart / EGS) up to eg_len − 1, where eg_len is defined as vl / EGS.
RULE 177: The value of vstart determines which element groups of the destination register vd are processed, so that only groups with indices greater than or equal to vstart / EGS are updated.
RULE 178: The vstart register supplies the lower bound of the element index for vector‑element processing.
RULE 179: The vstart register determines the first vector element that a vector instruction will operate on.
RULE 180: For the vandn instruction, processing iterates over each vector element i such that i ≥ vstart and i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 181: Execution of a vector instruction proceeds sequentially from the element indexed by vstart up to the element indexed by vl‑1.
RULE 182: The vstart register determines the first element index processed by the vbrev.v instruction.
RULE 183: During execution of vbrev.v, the instruction processes element indices i starting at the value held in vstart and continuing up to vl‑1 inclusive.
RULE 184: The range of element indices processed by vbrev.v is defined as i ∈ [vstart, vl‑1], where vl is the current vector length.
RULE 185: The vstart CSR determines the lower bound of the element index loop for a vector instruction such as vbrev8.v.
RULE 186: A vector instruction iterates over element indices i starting at the value of vstart and continuing while i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 187: The set of element indices processed by a vector instruction is exactly those i that satisfy vstart ≤ i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 188: When vstart = 0, the vector instruction begins processing with element index 0.
RULE 189: When vstart = vl, the vector instruction’s element loop body is not entered and no element updates are performed.
RULE 190: The vstart register defines the starting element index for the execution of a vector instruction.
RULE 191: A vector instruction processes elements whose indices are greater than or equal to the value in vstart and less than or equal to vl‑1.
RULE 192: The vstart register provides the lower bound of the element index range, while vl‑1 provides the upper bound for vector instruction execution.
RULE 193: The vstart register specifies the lower bound of the element index range for vector instruction execution.
RULE 194: For every vector instruction, the element‑wise loop iterates from index vstart up to vl‑1 inclusive.
RULE 195: An element is processed by a vector instruction only if its index i satisfies vstart ≤ i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 196: Elements with indices lower than the value of vstart are never processed by the vector instruction.
RULE 197: The vstart register supplies the lower bound index for the element range processed by a vector instruction, determining the first element on which the instruction operates.
RULE 198: During execution of the vclz.v instruction, the hardware iterates over element indices i such that i ranges from the value in vstart up to vl‑1 inclusive.
RULE 199: The iteration performed by vclz.v includes every element whose index i satisfies i ≥ vstart and i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 200: If the value of vstart is equal to vl, the vclz.v loop executes zero iterations and produces no element results.
RULE 201: If the value of vstart is greater than vl‑1, the vclz.v loop executes zero iterations and produces no element results.
RULE 202: The vstart register supplies the lower bound for the element index that a vector instruction uses when iterating over vector elements.
RULE 203: For a vector instruction, the element indices processed begin at the value contained in vstart and continue sequentially up to vl-1, where vl is the current vector length.
RULE 204: The hardware processes vector elements for a vector instruction by iterating index i from the current value of vstart up to vl‑1, where vl is the current vector length.
RULE 205: Elements whose indices are lower than the current value of vstart are not operated on by the vector instruction.
RULE 206: When executing the vghsh.vv instruction, the architectural register vstart is read and its value is divided by the element‑group size (EGS) to compute eg_start = vstart / EGS.
RULE 207: The eg_start value derived from vstart determines the first element‑group processed by the vghsh.vv instruction, causing the loop to iterate over element groups i from eg_start up to eg_len‑1, where eg_len = vl / EGS.
RULE 208: The vstart register supplies the starting element‑group offset for a vector instruction.
RULE 209: For a vector instruction, the starting element‑group index is computed as the integer division of vstart by the instruction’s element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 210: The value held in vstart determines the first element group that the vector instruction processes.
RULE 211: The execution loop of a vector instruction iterates from the element‑group index derived from vstart up to the index eg_len‑1.
RULE 212: The vstart register holds the index of the first vector element that a vector instruction will process.
RULE 213: When a vector instruction executes, it processes elements in order from the index given by vstart up to the index vl‑1, where vl is the current vector length.
RULE 214: The first element processed by a vector instruction has index equal to the current value of vstart.
RULE 215: Any vector element with an index lower than the value in vstart is not processed by that vector instruction.
RULE 216: For VROL (vector rotate left) instructions, the hardware iterates over element indices starting at the value held in the vstart register and continuing sequentially up to vl − 1.
RULE 217: The vstart register defines the lower bound of the element‑index range processed by VROL instructions, so any element whose index is less than the value in vstart is not operated on.
RULE 218: All vector elements whose indices are between vstart and vl − 1 inclusive are processed exactly once by VROL instructions.
RULE 219: The vstart register supplies the lower bound for the element index used by any vector‑rotate‑right (vror) instruction.
RULE 220: For each vror form (vector‑vector, vector‑scalar, vector‑immediate), the instruction iterates over element indices i starting at the current vstart value and continuing while i ≤ vl‑1.
RULE 221: Elements whose indices are less than the current vstart value are never read or written by a vror instruction; processing begins at the element indexed by vstart.
RULE 222: The upper bound of the vror element loop is the current vl register value minus one, therefore the number of elements processed by the instruction equals vl minus vstart.
RULE 223: The architectural register vstart determines the starting element-group index eg_start by integer division of vstart by the instruction's element-group size (EGS), i.e., eg_start = vstart / EGS.
RULE 224: For a vector-SHA-2 instruction, the element-wise loop begins at the element-group index computed from vstart (eg_start) and iterates up to, but not including, eg_len = vl / EGS.
RULE 225: The vector-SHA-2 instruction processes only those element groups whose indices are greater than or equal to vstart / EGS, and it skips all element groups with lower indices.
RULE 226: The observable result of a vector-SHA-2 instruction must be identical to the result obtained by executing the specified algorithm on the subset of element groups starting at the element-group that contains the element indexed by vstart.
RULE 227: The architectural vstart register supplies the starting element index for a vector instruction.
RULE 228: The element‑group loop start index eg_start is defined as the integer division of vstart by the instruction’s element‑group size (EGS).
RULE 229: The element‑group loop iterates over element groups i from the start computed from vstart (eg_start) up to eg_len−1, where eg_len equals the integer division of the current vector length vl by EGS.
RULE 230: The value stored in vstart determines the first element group that a vector instruction operates on.
RULE 231: A non‑zero vstart causes a vector instruction to resume execution at the corresponding element group after an exception or trap.
RULE 232: vstart is a CSR that supplies the vector start index for a vector SM3‑C instruction.
RULE 233: For a vector SM3‑C instruction, the starting element‑group index eg_start is calculated as the integer division of vstart by the element‑group size EGS.
RULE 234: A vector SM3‑C instruction iterates over element groups with loop variable i beginning at (vstart/EGS) and continuing while i < (vl/EGS), thereby processing groups from eg_start up to eg_len‑1.
RULE 235: Any vector element with an index smaller than the value held in vstart is excluded from processing by the vector SM3‑C instruction.
RULE 236: The vstart CSR holds a count that is used to calculate the starting element‑group index of a vector instruction.
RULE 237: When a vector instruction such as vsm3me.vv is executed, the hardware reads the value of vstart and divides it by the element‑group size (EGS) to compute the loop start index.
RULE 238: The computed loop start index is defined as eg_start = (vstart / EGS) using integer division.
RULE 239: The execution loop of the vector instruction begins iteration with i equal to the value of vstart divided by EGS.
RULE 240: The execution loop iterates from the start index derived from vstart up to eg_len‑1, where eg_len is the total number of element groups.
RULE 241: The total number of element groups eg_len is calculated as (vl / EGS) and together with vstart defines the loop’s termination condition.
RULE 242: The value held in vstart therefore determines the element‑group position in the vector registers at which the instruction begins processing elements.
RULE 243: The element‑group start index used by the instruction is computed as eg_start = (vstart / EGS), where the division is integer division and EGS is the instruction’s element‑group size.
RULE 244: The instruction’s processing loop iterates over element‑group indices i from (vstart / EGS) up to eg_len − 1, where eg_len = (vl / EGS) and vl is the current vector length.
RULE 245: The value contained in the vstart register determines which element‑group is the first one processed by the instruction.
RULE 246: When the vsm4r instruction is executed, the hardware must compute eg_start as the integer division of the current vstart value by the element‑group size EGS.
RULE 247: The integer division of vstart by EGS yields the index of the first element group that the vsm4r instruction will process.
RULE 248: The foreach loop that implements vsm4r must iterate from i = eg_start to i = eg_len‑1, so the value stored in vstart determines the loop’s starting element‑group index.
RULE 249: The division of the byte‑addressed vstart value by EGS must convert that byte offset into an element‑group index used by the vector operation.
RULE 250: For VWSLL instructions, the element‑wise computation loop iterates over indices i starting from the current vstart CSR value up to vl‑1 inclusive.
RULE 251: A VWSLL instruction processes only those vector elements whose index i satisfies vstart ≤ i < vl.
RULE 252: The value contained in the vstart CSR at the moment of execution determines the first element index on which a VWSLL instruction operates.
RULE 253: The vector‑extension CSR vstart is part of the architectural state defined by the RISC‑V vector extension.
RULE 254: The mstatus register’s VS field encodes the status of the vector‑extension state, and this encoding includes the vstart CSR.
RULE 255: The presence and dirty/clean/initial/off status of the vstart CSR are reflected in the VS field’s WARL encoding using the values Off, Initial, Clean, and Dirty.
RULE 256: In M-mode, accessing vstart register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x008; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 257: In S-mode, accessing vstart register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x008; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 258: In U-mode, accessing vstart register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x008; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 259: In M-mode, setting vstart.VALUE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x008, a0; csrr a1, 0x008; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 260: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the vstart register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x008, a0; csrr a1, 0x008; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 261: In M-mode, accessing vstart register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0x008; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 262: In M-mode, accessing vstart register while Zvkg extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zvkg; csrr a0, 0x008; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: vtype
NUMBER: 0xc21
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: MXLEN
FIELDS: 63..63=VILL, 7..7=VMA, 6..6=VTA, 5..3=VSEW, 2..0=VLMUL
EXTENSIONS: V, Zvkt
RULE 1: The vtype CSR is located at CSR address 0xC21.
RULE 2: The vtype CSR has URO privilege, allowing reads in unprivileged mode but prohibiting any writes.
RULE 3: An attempt to write the vtype CSR causes an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 4: The vtype CSR contains the vector data type configuration for the current hart.
RULE 5: The vtype CSR is one of the seven unprivileged CSRs introduced by the RISC‑V vector extension.
RULE 6: The vtype CSR is classified as the vector data type register.
RULE 7: The vtype CSR is an XLEN‑wide, read‑only register whose architectural value can be changed only by the vset{i}vl{i} instructions and cannot be modified by any other instruction.
RULE 8: The vtype register provides the default vector type that defines how elements are organized within each vector register, how vector registers are grouped, and how masked‑off elements and elements beyond the current vector length are treated in vector results.
RULE 9: The most‑significant bit of vtype (bit XLEN‑1, named vill) indicates an illegal vector‑type value, and implementations must raise an illegal‑value trap whenever this bit is set.
RULE 10: Bits XLEN‑2 down to 8 of vtype are reserved and must be written with zero; any non‑zero value in this field is treated as reserved.
RULE 11: Bit 7 of vtype (vma) is the vector‑mask‑agnostic flag that controls whether vector operations ignore mask bits.
RULE 12: Bit 6 of vtype (vta) is the vector‑tail‑agnostic flag that controls whether vector operations ignore tail elements past the current vector length.
RULE 13: Bits 5 down to 3 of vtype (vsew[2:0]) select the selected element width (SEW) for vector operations.
RULE 14: Bits 2 down to 0 of vtype (vlmul[2:0]) select the vector‑register‑group multiplier (LMUL) for vector operations.
RULE 15: Implementations may internally represent the illegal‑value condition indicated by the vill bit by using an illegal 64‑bit combination in the vsew[1:0] field instead of storing a separate vill bit.
RULE 16: For implementations with ELEN = 32, the architectural state of vtype can be reduced to the seven bits that encode vma, vta, vsew[1:0], and vlmul[2:0].
RULE 17: The vtype CSR exists to allow the vector ISA to be encoded within a 32‑bit instruction, and the vset{i}vl{i} instruction sets both the vector length (vl) and the vtype fields before any vector instruction executes.
RULE 18: Implementations may fuse a vset{i}vl{i} instruction with subsequent vector instructions into a single internal micro‑operation, reusing the same vl and vtype values across multiple vector instructions.
RULE 19: Future standard or custom vector extensions may add new fields to vtype, extending its existing layout to support additional data types.
RULE 20: After any write to the vtype CSR, software must read the vill bit in vtype.
RULE 21: If the vill bit in vtype is set after a write, the configuration encoded in vtype is not supported and software must follow an alternate code path.
RULE 22: The vsew[2:0] field of vtype encodes the selected element width (SEW) for subsequent vector instructions according to the mapping 000->8, 001->16, 010->32, 011->64, and any 1xx pattern is reserved.
RULE 23: Writing a value to vtype sets the SEW for all later vector operations based on the vsew[2:0] mapping defined for vtype.
RULE 24: The 1xx encodings of the vsew[2:0] field in vtype are reserved and must not be used to select an SEW.
RULE 25: The vill bit in vtype must be checked after any modification of vtype fields, including changes to vsew or LMUL, to confirm that the selected configuration is supported by the implementation.
RULE 26: The vtype CSR contains a signed 3‑bit field vlmul[2:0] whose value determines LMUL as LMUL = 2^(signed vlmul[2:0]).
RULE 27: Any vtype encoding that would produce an LMUL value smaller than SEWMIN/ELEN is defined as reserved.
RULE 28: Implementations that do not support LMUL values less than SEWMIN/ELEN may set the vill (illegal‑value) flag in the vtype CSR for those encodings.
RULE 29: Implementations are not required to set the vill flag for vtype encodings that produce LMUL < SEWMIN/ELEN, allowing future extensions to define behavior.
RULE 30: Assemblers are recommended to emit a warning, not an error, when a vsetvli instruction attempts to write an LMUL smaller than SEWMIN/ELEN into the vtype CSR.
RULE 31: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 100, the encoding is reserved and does not define a legal LMUL.
RULE 32: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 001, the LMUL derived from vtype is 1/8.
RULE 33: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 001, vtype defines 32 register groups.
RULE 34: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 001, the maximum number of elements (VLMAX) for that vtype configuration is VLEN/SEW/8.
RULE 35: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 001, each register group defined by vtype consists of a single vector register v n.
RULE 36: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 010, the LMUL derived from vtype is 1/4.
RULE 37: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 010, vtype defines 32 register groups.
RULE 38: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 010, the maximum number of elements (VLMAX) for that vtype configuration is VLEN/SEW/4.
RULE 39: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 010, each register group defined by vtype consists of a single vector register v n.
RULE 40: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 011, the LMUL derived from vtype is 1/2.
RULE 41: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 011, vtype defines 32 register groups.
RULE 42: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 011, the maximum number of elements (VLMAX) for that vtype configuration is VLEN/SEW/2.
RULE 43: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 011, each register group defined by vtype consists of a single vector register v n.
RULE 44: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 000, the LMUL derived from vtype is 1.
RULE 45: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 000, vtype defines 32 register groups.
RULE 46: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 000, the maximum number of elements (VLMAX) for that vtype configuration is VLEN/SEW.
RULE 47: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 000, each register group defined by vtype consists of a single vector register v n.
RULE 48: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 101, the LMUL derived from vtype is 2.
RULE 49: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 101, vtype defines 16 register groups.
RULE 50: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 101, the maximum number of elements (VLMAX) for that vtype configuration is 2 × VLEN/SEW.
RULE 51: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 101, each register group defined by vtype consists of the consecutive registers v n and v n+1.
RULE 52: When a register group for LMUL = 2 defined by vtype starts with an odd‑numbered register, that group encoding is reserved.
RULE 53: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 110, the LMUL derived from vtype is 4.
RULE 54: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 110, vtype defines 8 register groups.
RULE 55: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 110, the maximum number of elements (VLMAX) for that vtype configuration is 4 × VLEN/SEW.
RULE 56: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 110, each register group defined by vtype consists of the consecutive registers v n through v n+3.
RULE 57: When a register group for LMUL = 4 defined by vtype starts with a register number that is not a multiple of four, that group encoding is reserved.
RULE 58: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 111, the LMUL derived from vtype is 8.
RULE 59: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 111, vtype defines 4 register groups.
RULE 60: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 111, the maximum number of elements (VLMAX) for that vtype configuration is 8 × VLEN/SEW.
RULE 61: When the vlmul[2:0] field of vtype equals 111, each register group defined by vtype consists of the consecutive registers v n through v n+7.
RULE 62: When a register group for LMUL = 8 defined by vtype starts with a register number that is not a multiple of eight, that group encoding is reserved.
RULE 63: Mask registers are always contained in a single vector register regardless of the LMUL value encoded in vtype.
RULE 64: The vtype CSR contains the two architectural bits vta and vma.
RULE 65: The vta bit in vtype determines whether destination tail elements are handled as undisturbed when vta=0 or as agnostic when vta=1.
RULE 66: The vma bit in vtype determines whether destination inactive masked‑off elements are handled as undisturbed when vma=0 or as agnostic when vma=1.
RULE 67: When vtype has vta=0 and vma=0, both tail elements and inactive masked‑off elements follow the undisturbed policy.
RULE 68: When vtype has vta=0 and vma=1, tail elements follow the undisturbed policy and inactive masked‑off elements follow the agnostic policy.
RULE 69: When vtype has vta=1 and vma=0, tail elements follow the agnostic policy and inactive masked‑off elements follow the undisturbed policy.
RULE 70: When vtype has vta=1 and vma=1, both tail elements and inactive masked‑off elements follow the agnostic policy.
RULE 71: Mask‑destination tail elements are always treated as if vta=1 regardless of the actual vta setting in vtype.
RULE 72: Under the undisturbed policy selected via vtype, each affected destination element retains the value it held before the instruction.
RULE 73: Under the agnostic policy selected via vtype, each affected destination element may either retain its previous value or be overwritten with all‑1s, with the choice possibly varying per element and not required to be deterministic across identical executions.
RULE 74: When the agnostic policy is applied via vtype, any overwritten value must be all‑1s rather than all‑0s.
RULE 75: An in‑order implementation may ignore the vta and vma bits in vtype and always apply the undisturbed policy, provided it still writes the bits to vtype for compatibility.
RULE 76: An out‑of‑order implementation may treat tail elements as undisturbed while keeping mask elements agnostic even when vtype indicates both agnostic, if that simplifies the design.
RULE 77: The vtype CSR must contain the vta and vma bits so that software can read, write, and migrate thread state correctly.
RULE 78: The vsetvli instruction encodes the vta and vma bits of vtype using the flags ta (set vta=1), tu (set vta=0), ma (set vma=1), and mu (set vma=0).
RULE 79: The assembly encoding vsetvli ... ta, ma sets vtype bits vta=1 and vma=1; tu, ma sets vta=0 and vma=1; ta, mu sets vta=1 and vma=0; and tu, mu sets vta=0 and vma=0.
RULE 80: Prior to specification version 0.9 omitted vsetvli flags caused vtype to default to vta=0 and vma=0, but this defaulting behavior is now deprecated and current assembly must explicitly set the flags, thereby defining the vtype bits.
RULE 81: The vtype CSR includes a vill (vector‑type‑illegal) bit that records whether a prior vsetvl instruction attempted to write an unsupported value.
RULE 82: The vill bit is located in the most‑significant bit position of vtype, i.e., bit XLEN‑1.
RULE 83: Because the vill bit occupies the sign bit of vtype, software can test its state with a branch on the sign bit.
RULE 84: When the vill bit in vtype is 1, every vector instruction whose operation depends on the contents of vtype raises an illegal‑instruction exception.
RULE 85: When the vill bit in vtype is 1, vsetvl instructions are exception‑free because they do not depend on vtype.
RULE 86: When the vill bit in vtype is 1, whole‑register vector load and store instructions are exception‑free because they do not depend on vtype.
RULE 87: When the vill bit in vtype is 1, all other bits of vtype (bits XLEN‑2 down to 0) shall be zero, so the CSR appears as a sign‑extended zero value with only the vill bit set.
RULE 88: After a hardware reset, the vtype register must be in a state that can be read and then restored with a single vsetvl instruction.
RULE 89: The recommended reset configuration sets the vill field of vtype to 1.
RULE 90: The recommended reset configuration clears all bits of vtype other than the vill field to zero.
RULE 91: The reset values for vtype are recommended but not mandatory, yet the architecture requires that the reset state of vtype be restorable by a single vsetvl instruction.
RULE 92: Software must modify the vtype register dynamically when operating on multiple vectors that use different precision values.
RULE 93: The dynamic modification of vtype must preserve the SEW and LMUL fields unchanged.
RULE 94: Keeping the SEW and LMUL fields constant through vtype guarantees that the VLMAX value remains constant.
RULE 95: When source and destination vector registers overlap and have different effective element widths, the instruction is mask‑agnostic regardless of the value of the vma bit in the vtype register.
RULE 96: When source and destination vector registers overlap and have different effective element widths, the instruction is tail‑agnostic regardless of the value of the vta bit in the vtype register.
RULE 97: The vtype register includes a vma bit that selects the masking policy for elements that are masked‑off in a vector instruction.
RULE 98: When the vtype vma bit selects the mask‑undisturbed policy, masked‑off destination elements retain their previous architectural values.
RULE 99: When the vtype vma bit selects the mask‑agnostic policy, masked‑off destination elements are written as if the mask were irrelevant.
RULE 100: The masking behavior of any vector instruction that depends on the vtype vma setting is applied according to the value of that bit.
RULE 101: The vtype register includes a vta (vector tail‑agnostic) bit that normally controls whether tail elements of a vector operation are treated as agnostic or undisturbed.
RULE 102: Mask destination values are always treated as tail‑agnostic regardless of the setting of the vtype vta bit.
RULE 103: When vtype.vma = 1, a vector instruction may overwrite inactive elements (elements whose mask bit is disabled) with the value 1.
RULE 104: When vtype.vma = 0, inactive elements do not raise exceptions and the instruction does not modify any destination vector register group for those elements.
RULE 105: When vtype.vta = 1, a vector instruction may overwrite tail elements (elements with indices ≥ vl) with the value 1, or with the instruction’s result for mask‑producing instructions except mask loads.
RULE 106: When vtype.vta = 0, tail elements do not raise exceptions and the instruction does not modify any destination vector register group for those elements.
RULE 107: The vtype register is a control‑and‑status register that holds the vector configuration settings for the processor.
RULE 108: Each execution of a vector‑configuration instruction (vsetvli, vsetivli, or vsetvl) overwrites the vtype register with the value supplied by the instruction’s vtypei operand or rs2 source operand.
RULE 109: The hardware determines the actual vector length placed in the vl register based on the current value of the vtype register together with the microarchitectural implementation and the requested application vector length.
RULE 110: For every subsequent vector instruction, the hardware consults the current value of the vtype register to compute the effective vector length that will be used by that instruction.
RULE 111: If the vtype.vill bit (bit XLEN‑1) is set to 1, the vtype value is illegal and the implementation must treat the configuration as illegal.
RULE 112: All bits of vtype from XLEN‑2 down to bit 8 are reserved and must be zero; any non‑zero value in these bits has no defined architectural effect.
RULE 113: When the vtype.vma bit (bit 7) is 1, the vector instruction is mask‑agnostic; when it is 0, the instruction is mask‑sensitive.
RULE 114: When the vtype.vta bit (bit 6) is 1, the vector instruction is tail‑agnostic; when it is 0, the instruction is tail‑sensitive.
RULE 115: The vtype.vsew[2:0] field (bits 5‑3) encodes the selected element width (SEW) and determines the element size used by subsequent vector instructions.
RULE 116: The vtype.vlmul[2:0] field (bits 2‑0) encodes the vector‑register‑group multiplier (LMUL) and determines how many vector registers constitute a logical vector group.
RULE 117: The described vtype layout applies to RV32, and for any XLEN the vtype.vill field remains at bit XLEN‑1 with the same semantics.
RULE 118: A new vtype value is supplied to the hardware via the immediate fields of the vsetvli and vsetivli instructions.
RULE 119: The vsetvl instruction obtains the vtype value from its rs2 register operand, allowing software to restore a previously saved vtype configuration.
RULE 120: If a value written to vtype is not supported by the implementation, the vill bit in vtype is set, all other bits in vtype are cleared to zero, and the vl register is set to zero.
RULE 121: A vtype value that has the vill bit set is defined as an unsupported configuration.
RULE 122: Implementations must examine every bit of a vtype value to determine support, and the presence of any unsupported bit in any location of vtype must cause vill to be set.
RULE 123: For the vsetvl instruction, the implementation must check the entire XLEN‑wide vtype argument and may not ignore any bits, ensuring that unsupported vector features in vtype cause a trap.
RULE 124: An implementation is allowed to trap on a write of an illegal vtype value instead of setting vill, providing a mechanism for forward‑compatible emulation of new configurations.
RULE 125: Software can determine whether a particular vector unit configuration is supported by testing that vill in vtype remains clear after writing the desired vtype value.
RULE 126: When a vsetvli or vsetvl instruction is executed with rd = x0 and rs1 = x0, the implementation may update the vtype register while preserving the current vl value, provided that the resulting VLMAX is not reduced.
RULE 127: If the new SEW/LMUL ratio implied by an updated vtype would cause VLMAX to shrink, the vsetvli/vsetvl form with rd = x0 and rs1 = x0 is reserved and must not be used.
RULE 128: After a vtype change performed by the rd = x0, rs1 = x0 form, vl must remain a legal value for the current vtype setting.
RULE 129: When a vsetvli or vsetvl instruction is executed with rd ≠ x0 and rs1 = x0 (AVL = maximum unsigned integer), the implementation may change the vtype register and must write the resulting VLMAX to both vl and the destination register rd.
RULE 130: An implementation may set the vill (illegal‑vtype) bit whenever the vtype register is changed, but setting vill does not modify the normative behavior of vtype updates specified in the other rules.
RULE 131: The vset{i}vl{i} instructions set VLMAX based on the vtype argument supplied to the instruction.
RULE 132: The architectural vtype register determines the maximum vector length (VLMAX) that is used for subsequent vector‑length (vl) calculations.
RULE 133: The VLMAX value derived from the vtype register (or vtype argument) remains in effect for all following vl calculations until a later vset{i}vl{i} instruction or a write to the vtype register changes it.
RULE 134: The vtype register holds the current vector type configuration, encoding the selected SEW, LMUL, and the tail‑aggr policy bits ta and ma.
RULE 135: Executing a vsetvli instruction with SEW e16, LMUL m4, ta, and ma updates vtype so that the processor interprets vector registers as 16‑bit integer vectors.
RULE 136: Executing a vsetvli instruction with SEW e32, LMUL m8, ta, and ma updates vtype so that the processor interprets vector registers as 32‑bit values.
RULE 137: Every vsetvli instruction rewrites vtype to reflect the newly requested vector configuration.
RULE 138: The vtype register contains a field named vma that controls vector‑mask‑agnostic behavior.
RULE 139: When vtype.vma equals 1, masked vector load instructions must update inactive elements in the destination vector‑register group.
RULE 140: When vtype.vma equals 0, masked vector load instructions must not update inactive elements in the destination vector‑register group.
RULE 141: For unit‑stride and constant‑stride vector memory operations, the vtype register does not need to be updated because the effective element width is encoded directly in the instruction.
RULE 142: For indexed vector memory operations, the SEW and LMUL fields of the vtype register specify the data width of the loaded or stored elements.
RULE 143: For indexed vector memory operations, the effective element width of the index elements is taken from the instruction, not from the vtype register.
RULE 144: The vtype register contains fields that hold the SEW (standard element width) and LMUL values.
RULE 145: For vector indexed load and store instructions, the SEW and LMUL values stored in vtype determine the size of the data elements accessed.
RULE 146: The vtype register includes a field named vill.
RULE 147: The vill field in vtype is consulted by the vector mask‑load (vlm.v) and vector mask‑store (vsm.v) instructions to determine their operation.
RULE 148: The operation of vlm.v and vsm.v is influenced by vtype through the constraints that vtype imposes on the effective vector length (vl).
RULE 149: When a whole‑register load or store instruction is executed, the effective vector length (evl) is calculated as evl = NFIELDS * VLEN / EEW, and this calculation is performed without consulting the vtype CSR.
RULE 150: Whole‑register load and store instructions ignore the current value of the vtype register, so changes to vtype have no effect on their operation.
RULE 151: The reduction tree used by unordered single‑width floating‑point sum‑reduction instructions must be deterministic for each specific value held in the vtype register together with the current vector length (vl).
RULE 152: The architectural behavior of the reduction tree for unordered single‑width floating‑point sum‑reduction instructions is defined solely by the present contents of the vtype register in conjunction with the current vector length.
RULE 153: The vtype architectural register contains a field named vlmul.
RULE 154: Vector mask‑register logical instructions operate on a single vector register independent of the vlmul field setting in vtype.
RULE 155: Execution of vector mask‑register logical instructions does not modify the vlmul field of vtype.
RULE 156: The vtype CSR includes a field named vma.
RULE 157: When executing a masked viota.m instruction, the vma field of the vtype register is defined to be 0.
RULE 158: The vtype register contains a SEW field.
RULE 159: The vtype SEW field is used to indicate or calculate the Effective Element Size (EEW) for one or more operands of an element‑group instruction.
RULE 160: When an operand is an element group, the vtype SEW field and the resulting EEW refer to the number of bits in each individual element within the group, not to the total number of bits in the whole group.
RULE 161: The vtype register contains an LMUL field that supplies the LMUL value used by the hardware.
RULE 162: The LMUL setting in the vtype register is used to indicate or calculate the effective length multiplier (EMUL) for one or more operands of a vector instruction.
RULE 163: The data‑independent execution latency defined for the Zvkt extension must not be applied to the vtype register, which is treated as a control value exempt from DIEL.
RULE 164: vtype is a control‑and‑status register that is defined by the RISC‑V vector extension.
RULE 165: The vector‑extension state encoded by the VS field of the mstatus register includes the CSR vtype.
RULE 166: The vector‑extension state comprises the vector registers v0‑v31 and the CSRs vcsr, vxrm, vxsat, vstart, vl, vtype, and vlenb.
RULE 167: In M-mode, accessing vtype register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0xc21; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 168: In S-mode, accessing vtype register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0xc21; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 169: In U-mode, accessing vtype register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0xc21; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 170: In M-mode, setting vtype.VILL (bit 63) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8000000000000000; csrs 0xc21, a0; csrr a1, 0xc21; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8000000000000000].
RULE 171: In M-mode, setting vtype.VMA (bit 7) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x80) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x80; csrs 0xc21, a0; csrr a1, 0xc21; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x80].
RULE 172: In M-mode, setting vtype.VTA (bit 6) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x40) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x40; csrs 0xc21, a0; csrr a1, 0xc21; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x40].
RULE 173: In M-mode, setting vtype.VSEW (bit 3) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x8) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x8; csrs 0xc21, a0; csrr a1, 0xc21; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x8].
RULE 174: In M-mode, setting vtype.VLMUL (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0xc21, a0; csrr a1, 0xc21; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 175: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the vtype register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0xc21, a0; csrr a1, 0xc21; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 176: In M-mode, accessing vtype register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0xc21; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 177: In M-mode, accessing vtype register while Zvkt extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: Zvkt; csrr a0, 0xc21; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: vxrm
NUMBER: 0x00a
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: MXLEN
FIELDS: 63..0=VALUE
EXTENSIONS: V
RULE 1: vxrm is a CSR located at address 0x00A.
RULE 2: vxrm is accessible in User‑mode with read/write privileges (URW).
RULE 3: vxrm holds the vector fixed‑point rounding mode.
RULE 4: The rounding mode stored in vxrm controls how vector fixed‑point arithmetic instructions round their results.
RULE 5: The vector fixed-point rounding-mode register (vxrm) is a control-and-status register that stores a two-bit read/write field in its least-significant bits vxrm[1:0].
RULE 6: All bits of vxrm above bit 1 (vxrm[XLEN-1:2]) must be written with zeros.
RULE 7: The vxrm register has a unique CSR address that allows software to read or write it independently of other registers.
RULE 8: The current value of vxrm is also mirrored as a field inside the vcsr register.
RULE 9: A csrwi instruction can write a new rounding mode to vxrm while returning the previous vxrm value in the destination register.
RULE 10: The rounding mode encoded in vxrm determines the rounding increment r used by the fixed-point rounding algorithm.
RULE 11: When vxrm[1:0] equals 0b00 (round-to-nearest-up), the rounding increment r is equal to the bit v[d-1] of the pre-rounding value.
RULE 12: When vxrm[1:0] equals 0b01 (round-to-nearest-even), the rounding increment r is v[d-1] && (v[d-2:0] != 0 || v[d]).
RULE 13: When vxrm[1:0] equals 0b10 (round-down), the rounding increment r is zero.
RULE 14: When vxrm[1:0] equals 0b11 (round-to-odd), the rounding increment r is (!v[d]) && (v[d-1:0] != 0).
RULE 15: The fixed-point rounding result for a value v with d discarded bits is computed as (v >> d) plus the rounding increment r selected according to vxrm.
RULE 16: The roundoff_unsigned(v,d) function computes (unsigned(v) >> d) plus the rounding increment r defined by vxrm.
RULE 17: The roundoff_signed(v,d) function computes (signed(v) >> d) plus the rounding increment r defined by vxrm.
RULE 18: The vxrm register is a 2‑bit field that resides in bits 2 and 1 of the XLEN‑wide vector control and status CSR (vcsr).
RULE 19: The vxrm register defines the fixed‑point rounding mode used by vector operations.
RULE 20: The vxrm register can be accessed both as an independent CSR and as the vxrm field within the vcsr CSR.
RULE 21: Any write to the vxrm register via the independent CSR updates bits 2:1 of vcsr, and any read of vxrm through either CSR returns the same current architectural value.
RULE 22: The vxrm register shares the same architectural state regardless of whether it is accessed through the independent CSR or through the vcsr register.
RULE 23: After a reset, the value of the vxrm control‑and‑status register may be arbitrary.
RULE 24: Executing a vsetvl or vsetivl instruction does not modify the vxrm register.
RULE 25: Software must explicitly write a defined value to the vxrm register before using the vector unit.
RULE 26: The vxrm register holds a rounding‑mode setting that determines how the result of any vector averaging‑add or averaging‑subtract instruction is rounded after the mandatory one‑bit right shift.
RULE 27: For every averaging‑add (vaaddu, vaadd) and averaging‑subtract (vasubu, vasub) instruction, the value produced after infinite‑precision computation and a one‑bit right shift is rounded according to the setting in vxrm.
RULE 28: The rounding‑mode value stored in vxrm is used identically for both unsigned and signed variants of the vector averaging instructions.
RULE 29: In a vasub instruction, overflow can occur only when the smallest operand is subtracted from the largest operand and the vxrm setting specifies either round‑to‑nearest‑up (rnu) or round‑to‑nearest‑even (rne).
RULE 30: In signed fractional‑multiply‑with‑rounding‑and‑saturation instructions, the bits discarded by the right‑shift of the 2 × SEW product are rounded according to the current setting of the vxrm register.
RULE 31: The vxrm register selects the rounding mode (e.g., round‑to‑nearest, round‑down, round‑up, or round‑toward‑zero) that the hardware applies when rounding fractional‑multiply results.
RULE 32: The rounding operation governed by the vxrm register is performed before any saturation is applied to the final SEW‑sized result.
RULE 33: The architectural behavior of fractional‑multiply‑with‑rounding instructions depends on the value stored in the vxrm register, and this value directly determines how the discarded bits are rounded regardless of later saturation outcomes.
RULE 34: For all vector scaling shift‑right instructions, both logical (vssrl) and arithmetic (vssra), the bits that are shifted out are rounded based on the current value of the vxrm register.
RULE 35: The logical scaling right‑shift forms vssrl.vv, vssrl.vx, and vssrl.vi perform rounding by invoking the roundoff_unsigned function, which uses the current setting of the vxrm register.
RULE 36: The arithmetic scaling right‑shift forms vssra.vv, vssra.vx, and vssra.vi perform rounding by invoking the roundoff_signed function, which uses the current setting of the vxrm register.
RULE 37: The vxrm CSR holds the rounding mode that is applied by the vector narrowing fixed‑point clip instructions vnclipu and vnclip.
RULE 38: The rounding mode specified in vxrm controls rounding around the least‑significant bit of the destination element and is applied before any saturation occurs.
RULE 39: The vxrm register is a Control‑Status Register (CSR) defined by the RISC‑V vector extension.
RULE 40: The vxrm register belongs to the vector‑extension architectural state together with vector registers v0‑v31 and the CSRs vcsr, vxsat, vstart, vl, vtype, and vlenb.
RULE 41: The state of the vxrm CSR is reflected in the VS field of the mstatus register.
RULE 42: In M-mode, accessing vxrm register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x00a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 43: In S-mode, accessing vxrm register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x00a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 44: In U-mode, accessing vxrm register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x00a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 45: In M-mode, setting vxrm.VALUE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x00a, a0; csrr a1, 0x00a; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 46: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the vxrm register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x00a, a0; csrr a1, 0x00a; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 47: In M-mode, accessing vxrm register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0x00a; # Result: mcause = 0x0].

REGISTER: vxsat
NUMBER: 0x009
MODE: U-mode
WIDTH: MXLEN
FIELDS: 63..0=VALUE
EXTENSIONS: V
RULE 1: The vxsat CSR is located at architectural address 0x009.
RULE 2: The vxsat CSR is readable and writable from user mode, having URW (unprivileged read/write) privilege.
RULE 3: The vxsat CSR is an unprivileged CSR, so no higher privilege level is required to access it.
RULE 4: The vxsat CSR holds the Fixed‑Point Saturate Flag used by vector operations.
RULE 5: The vxsat CSR is defined as part of the vector extension programmer’s model specification.
RULE 6: The vxsat CSR is a read‑write register.
RULE 7: The only architecturally defined field of vxsat is bit 0 (vxsat[0]).
RULE 8: vxsat[0] is set to 1 when a fixed‑point vector instruction saturates an output value and is cleared to 0 otherwise.
RULE 9: All bits of vxsat from XLEN‑1 down to 1 are reserved, must be written as zeros, and read back as zeros.
RULE 10: Writes to vxsat[0] update the corresponding saturation‑flag bit in the vcsr register, mirroring the value.
RULE 11: Reads of the saturation‑flag bit from vcsr return the same value as vxsat[0].
RULE 12: The vxsat register is defined as an independent CSR that can also be accessed as a field of the XLEN‑bit‑wide vector control and status CSR vcsr.
RULE 13: Within the vcsr CSR, the vxsat field occupies bit position 0.
RULE 14: In the vcsr layout, bits 2:1 are allocated to the vxrm field and bit 0 is allocated to the vxsat field.
RULE 15: The vxsat field functions as a fixed‑point accrued saturation flag.
RULE 16: After reset, the vxsat CSR may contain an arbitrary value.
RULE 17: Software must explicitly set the vxsat field to a known state before using the vector unit.
RULE 18: The vxsat bit in the vector status register is set when a signed fractional multiply operation causes the result to saturate.
RULE 19: The vxsat bit is set only for vector single‑width fractional multiply instructions that round the product according to the current vxrm setting and then saturate the rounded value to fit into SEW bits.
RULE 20: If a signed fractional multiply instruction does not trigger the saturation condition, the vxsat bit is not set.
RULE 21: The vxsat register contains a single saturation flag bit.
RULE 22: Whenever any destination element of a vector narrowing fixed‑point clip instruction (vnclipu or vnclip) is saturated, the vxsat bit in the vxsat register is set.
RULE 23: vxsat is a vector‑extension Control‑and‑Status Register (CSR).
RULE 24: The architectural state of vxsat is tracked by the VS[1:0] field in the mstatus register.
RULE 25: The VS[1:0] field encodes the status of vxsat using the four values Off, Initial, Clean, and Dirty.
RULE 26: In M-mode, accessing vxsat register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; csrr a0, 0x009; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 27: In S-mode, accessing vxsat register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_supervisor; csrr a0, 0x009; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 28: In U-mode, accessing vxsat register must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; call machine_to_user; csrr a0, 0x009; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
RULE 29: In M-mode, setting vxsat.VALUE (bit 0) must be reflected on the readback (# Result: a2 = 0x1) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0x1; csrs 0x009, a0; csrr a1, 0x009; and a2, a0, a1; # Result: a2 = 0x1].
RULE 30: In M-mode, writing 0xffffffffffffffff to the vxsat register must not set it to 0xffffffff or 0xffffffffffffffff (# Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff) [# Extension Must Be Active: NONE; li a0, 0xffffffffffffffff; csrw 0x009, a0; csrr a1, 0x009; # Result: a1 != 0xffffffff & a1 != 0xffffffffffffffff].
RULE 31: In M-mode, accessing vxsat register while V extension is active must result in no exception (# Result: mcause = 0x0) [# Extension Must Be Active: V; csrr a0, 0x009; # Result: mcause = 0x0].
